input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
ISSUE=$(gh issue create --title "PROD: every /assets deep link 404s — nginx static-bundle location shadows the SPA route" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --label bug --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Impact (production, live now)

Every `/assets` and `/assets/<assetId>` **deep link, bookmark, and browser refresh 404s in production.** The whole ADR 0034 asset browse is reachable only by clicking through the SPA — share a link to an asset, or just hit refresh while on it, and it breaks.

Verified against prod:

```
GET /assets                → 301  →  http://<host>:8080/assets/   (!! downgrades https→http and leaks the internal container port)
GET /assets/               → 404
GET /assets/<uuid>         → 404
GET /suites   (control)    → 200
GET /results/<id> (control)→ 200
```

In a browser it's worse than a 404: Chrome follows that 301 to an unroutable `http://…:8080`, and the tab simply **hangs** until it times out.

## Root cause

`frontend/nginx.conf.template`. Vite emits its bundle to `dist/assets/`, which collides with the app's own `/assets` route. **Two independent mistakes**, either of which breaks it:

1. `location /assets/ { try_files $uri =404; }` — a *prefix* location claims the entire `/assets/*` path space, so `/assets/<uuid>` matched it and hit `=404` instead of falling through to the SPA `index.html`.
2. `location / { try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html; }` — the `$uri/` makes nginx find the on-disk `assets/` **directory** and issue its own 301 to `/assets/`, rebuilt from the *internal* scheme/port. That's where the `http://…:8080` comes from.

## Why the test suite didn't catch it

Structural blind spot: **prod serves the SPA from nginx; the Playwright e2e runs against Vite's dev server**, which has its own SPA fallback. So the e2e exercises `/assets` happily and can never see an nginx routing bug. In-app navigation looked perfect, which is exactly why this shipped.

## Fix
- Match the static bundle by **file extension** (`location ~* ^/assets/.+\.(js|css|…)$`), not by the `/assets/` prefix — a regex location still outranks `location /`, so the fingerprinted files keep their `immutable` long-cache.
- Drop `$uri/` from the SPA fallback — an SPA never wants directory-index redirects.

Verified against a real `vite build` behind real nginx: `/assets` + `/assets/<uuid>` → 200 SPA shell; real bundle file → 200 with `Cache-Control: public, immutable`; bogus bundle file → 404.

Guarded by `frontend/tests/nginxRouting.test.ts`, which pins both mistakes (mutation-checked: reverting either one fails the test).

Found by the ui-tester pass against prod after the #801–#805 deploy.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1)
echo "$ISSUE"
N=$(echo "$ISSUE" | grep -o '[0-9]*$')
git checkout -q -b fix/issue-$N-assets-deeplink-nginx
git add frontend/nginx.conf.template frontend/tests/nginxRouting.test.ts
git commit -q -m "$(cat <<EOF
fix(nginx): stop the static-bundle location shadowing the /assets SPA route

Every /assets and /assets/<assetId> deep link, bookmark and refresh 404'd in
production. Vite emits its bundle to dist/assets/, which collides with the app's
own /assets route, and two separate mistakes each broke it:

- \`location /assets/ { try_files \$uri =404; }\` claimed the whole /assets/* path
  space as a prefix, so /assets/<uuid> hit =404 instead of falling through to the
  SPA index.html. Match the bundle by FILE EXTENSION instead — a regex location
  still outranks \`location /\`, so the fingerprinted files keep their immutable
  long-cache.
- \`try_files \$uri \$uri/ /index.html\` made nginx find the on-disk assets/
  DIRECTORY and issue its own 301 to /assets/, rebuilt from the INTERNAL
  scheme/port — handing the browser http://<host>:8080/assets/, which is
  unroutable, so the tab hung rather than even 404ing. An SPA never wants
  directory-index redirects; drop \$uri/.

Verified against a real \`vite build\` behind real nginx: /assets and
/assets/<uuid> serve the SPA (200), the real bundle still 200s with
\`Cache-Control: public, immutable\`, and a bogus bundle file still 404s.

The e2e suite could never have caught this: prod serves the SPA from nginx, but
Playwright runs against Vite's dev server, which has its own SPA fallback. So the
guard is a config assertion on the template itself (nginxRouting.test.ts), pinning
both mistakes — mutation-checked, reverting either one fails it.

Fixes #$N

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
git push -u origin fix/issue-$N-assets-deeplink-nginx -q 2>&1 | tail -1
gh pr create --title "fix(nginx): stop the static-bundle location shadowing the /assets SPA route (#$N)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --body "Fixes #$N.

**Production is broken right now:** every \`/assets\` and \`/assets/<assetId>\` deep link, bookmark and browser-refresh 404s (and in a browser, *hangs* — nginx 301s to an unroutable \`http://<host>:8080/assets/\`). Only in-app SPA navigation works, which is why it shipped unnoticed.

Vite emits its bundle to \`dist/assets/\`, colliding with the app's own \`/assets\` route. Two independent mistakes:
1. \`location /assets/\` (a **prefix**) claimed the whole path space → \`/assets/<uuid>\` hit \`=404\`. Now matched by **file extension** via a regex location, which still outranks \`location /\`, so the bundle keeps its \`immutable\` long-cache.
2. \`try_files \$uri \$uri/ …\` found the on-disk \`assets/\` **directory** and 301'd to it, rebuilt from the internal scheme/port. Dropped \`\$uri/\` — an SPA never wants directory-index redirects.

**Verified against a real \`vite build\` behind real nginx:**
| route | before | after |
|---|---|---|
| \`/assets\` | 301 → hang | **200** (SPA shell) |
| \`/assets/<uuid>\` | 404 | **200** (SPA shell) |
| \`/assets/<real-bundle>.js\` | 200 | **200**, \`Cache-Control: public, immutable\` |
| \`/assets/nope.js\` | 404 | **404** |

**Why no test caught it, and the guard:** prod serves the SPA from **nginx**; the Playwright e2e runs against **Vite's dev server**, which has its own SPA fallback — so the e2e structurally *cannot* see an nginx routing bug. The guard is therefore a config assertion on the template (\`frontend/tests/nginxRouting.test.ts\`) pinning both mistakes. Mutation-checked: reverting either one fails 3 of its 4 tests.

Found by the ui-tester pass against prod after the #801–#805 deploy.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)" 2>&1 | tail -1

content.text: While CI runs, let me file the credential-leak issue properly — the ui-tester correctly argued it's **distinct** from #754 (different code path, different fix, and fixing #754 as scoped would not fix this).

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
gh issue create --title "P1: Iceberg SQL-catalog password is copied into assets.namespace and RENDERED IN THE UI (distinct from #754)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --label bug --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Impact

A live database password is **persisted into `assets.namespace`, served by `GET /api/v1/assets`, and rendered as plaintext label text in at least five UI surfaces** — the Dashboard "needs attention" list, the Assets tree (it is literally the *name of the top-level folder node*), the Assets flat table, and the asset detail subtitle **next to a one-click Copy button**.

Anyone who can see the dashboard can read (and copy) the credential. Confirmed live in prod, desktop and mobile.

## Root cause

`backend/app/services/asset_identity.py` → `_resolve_iceberg()` uses `connection.config.catalog_uri` **verbatim** as the OpenLineage namespace (the module docstring states this outright: `iceberg → {catalog_uri verbatim, or "file"}`).

For a **SQL-catalog** iceberg connection, `catalog_uri` embeds userinfo (`REDACTED`). So the password is copied byte-for-byte into `assets.namespace` at asset-materialization time, persisted, and then faithfully rendered by every consumer of the namespace.

## This is NOT covered by #754 — please do not close it as a duplicate

[#754](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/754) is scoped **exclusively to the connections read endpoint** ("...returned in plaintext by `GET /connections`"); its title, body and all four acceptance criteria are about connection config. It never mentions `assets`, `namespace`, or `resolve_asset_identity`.

Critically: **fixing #754 as written does not fix this.** Redacting `catalog_uri` on connection *read* changes nothing here, because `_resolve_iceberg` reads the raw config independently, on a different path, and keeps writing the credential into `assets.namespace`.

## Two further consequences (observed, not theorised)

1. **Egress to third parties.** The `LineageProvider` pull builds `nodeId=dataset:{namespace}:{name}` and sends it to the configured catalog **as a URL query parameter**. The password was therefore transmitted to Marquez inside a URL — the worst possible place for a secret, since catalogs log and index URLs.
2. **Plaintext in the observability backend.** The httpx client logged that request URL, so the credential is now sitting in App Insights `traces` in the clear. The structlog PII redactor doesn't catch it: it isn't a secret-*named* field, just a substring of a namespace.

## Suggested fix
- Sanitize at the **identity resolver**: strip userinfo when deriving the OL namespace (`scheme://host[:port]/path`). Do NOT rely on redacting at the read/render layer — the value must never be persisted in the first place.
- **Backfill/repair** the already-poisoned `assets.namespace` row(s).
- Rotate the exposed Postgres credential (same posture as the #536 PAT rotation precedent).
- Consider a redaction pass on the httpx request-URL log line as defence in depth.

Bonus: a credential-free namespace is also **stable across password rotation**. Today, rotating that password silently *forks the asset into a new namespace* — a new `assets` row, orphaning the old one along with its lineage and incidents. So this fix removes a second, quieter bug.

Found by the ui-tester pass against prod after the #801–#805 deploy. Evidence recorded on #754 as well.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

content.text:
The ui-tester ran, **and it earned its keep — it found two serious defects I'd missed.** Everything is now wound down again (re-verified after the redeploy).

## Mobile: yes, covered thoroughly

The pass ran **both** viewports. Mobile (390×844) results:

- **#801 overlay nav — PASS on all 4 criteria.** Measured `scrollWidth === clientWidth === 390` with the nav both open and shut (so content genuinely isn't squeezed), drawer renders as `role=dialog` over a scrim, scrim-tap closes without navigating, nav-item tap navigates *and* closes.
- **Zero horizontal page overflow on every single page** — Dashboard, Assets, Connections, Suites, suite detail, Results, run detail, Admin, Profile, Settings. Wide tables scroll inside their own containers, as designed.
- **#803 health split** renders cleanly at 390px.
- Zero console errors; zero unexpected 4xx/5xx.

## Two defects it caught

**1. PROD BLOCKER — every `/assets` deep link was 404. Fixed and shipped.**
`/assets` → 301 → **404**; `/assets/<uuid>` → **404**; in a browser it didn't even 404, it *hung* (nginx 301'd to an unroutable `http://<host>:8080`). `/suites` and `/results/<id>` were fine — so the entire asset browse was reachable only by clicking through the SPA. Refresh, bookmark, or share a link and it broke.

Root cause: Vite emits its bundle to `dist/assets/`, which collides with the app's own `/assets` route. Two independent mistakes in `nginx.conf.template` — a *prefix* `location /assets/` swallowing the path space, and `try_files $uri $uri/` triggering a directory-index 301. Fixed both (match the bundle by **file extension**; drop `$uri/`), verified against a real `vite build` behind real nginx, **filed #824 → PR #825 → merged → deployed → prod now returns 200** on both routes.

Why nothing caught it: **prod serves the SPA from nginx, but the Playwright e2e runs against Vite's dev server**, which has its own SPA fallback. The e2e *structurally cannot* see an nginx routing bug. So the guard is a config assertion on the template (`nginxRouting.test.ts`) — mutation-checked: reverting either mistake fails it.

**2. P1 security — the Iceberg DB password is rendered in the UI. Filed as #826.**
A live Postgres password is persisted into `assets.namespace` and shown as **plaintext label text** in five places — including as the *name of a tree folder node*, and on the asset detail **next to a Copy button**. Root cause: `_resolve_iceberg()` uses `catalog_uri` verbatim as the OL namespace.

The ui-tester made a correct call I want to flag: this is **not** a duplicate of #754. #754 is scoped to `GET /connections`, and fixing it as written would **not** fix this — `_resolve_iceberg` reads the raw config on a different path. Hence a separate issue. I also confirmed the credential egressed to Marquez inside a URL query param and landed in App Insights in plaintext.

**Recommend rotating that Postgres credential** (`dataq-pg-wus3-3erlgd`), same posture as the #536 PAT rotation. I have not touched it.

## Final Azure state (re-verified after redeploy)

- App: api / worker / frontend / redis **Running** on `43514a97`
- Harness: all 5 apps **Stopped** (incl. Marquez)
- All 7 cron jobs **Suspended**; both ADF triggers **Stopped**
- `dataq-app-migrate` Manual/Ready — the app's own job, never self-fires

Open follow-ups from this session: **#823** (OL identity alignment unproven), **#826** (credential in namespace, P1), **#754** (escalated with evidence).
rotate the password and resolve the security issues.

Two things here, and the order matters: **fix the code first**, so the new password doesn't just get re-leaked into `assets.namespace` the moment I rotate it. Let me map the blast radius before touching anything.

result.output:
=== #754 acceptance criteria ===
Found during the 2026-07-10 perf campaign, on prod.

The `iceberg` connection type (ADR 0030, Option A) carries **one** secret slot (`secret_property`). For a SQL catalog on Postgres the connection needs TWO credentials — the storage key and the catalog DB password — so the live `harness-iceberg` connection ended up with the Postgres password embedded in `config.catalog_uri` (`REDACTED`), and `GET /api/v1/connections` returns `config` verbatim: **every workspace user with connection read access can read the catalog DB password**. The 'secret is never returned on a connection' guarantee only covers the `secret`/`secret_ref` fields, not credentials smuggled into config by the connection author.

Remediation candidates (pick in-issue):
- Redact URI userinfo on read (`REDACTED`) — cheap, read-path only, but the value is still at rest in `connections.config` JSONB un-encrypted.
- Extend the iceberg config with a second secret slot (e.g. `catalog_secret_property` + `catalog_secret`) so the URI can ship credential-less — the honest fix for ADR 0030 §3.
- Validation guard: reject `catalog_uri` containing userinfo passwords at create/update time with a pointer to the secret slot (prevents the footgun for future connections).

**Ops action regardless of the fix:** rotate the harness Postgres admin password (`dataq-pg-wus3-3erlgd`) and re-point the `iceberg-catalog-uri` job secret + the `harness-iceberg` connection — the current password must be treated as exposed (readable via API by all workspace members since Flow D went live, and it surfaced in ops tooling output during this session).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A credential can no longer be read back out of `GET /connections` for the iceberg type (redaction and/or second secret slot)
- [ ] Create/update-time guard rejects passwords embedded in `catalog_uri`
- [ ] Harness PG password rotated + connection/job secrets re-pointed
- [ ] ADR 0030 §3 updated with the outcome

content.text:
I have the full picture. The honest fix (#754's own recommendation): give Iceberg a **second secret** so `catalog_uri` ships credential-less, plus a guard so nobody can smuggle one in again — and the protocol's rule ("adapters never touch the SecretStore; the caller resolves and hands in") is preserved by resolving it caller-side, generically.

Let me set up the plan and start.

content.text: Now the core change — a shared URI-credential helper plus the Iceberg second-secret slot.

input.content:
"""URI userinfo handling — keep credentials OUT of connection URIs (#754, #826).

A DSN-shaped URI (`REDACTED`) can smuggle a
password through any field that was only ever meant to hold a *location*. That is
exactly what happened to the Iceberg SQL-catalog `catalog_uri`: the connection type
has one secret slot (used by the storage key), so the catalog DB password ended up
inline in the URI — a *non-secret* config field. From there it was persisted to
`connections.config`, copied verbatim into `assets.namespace` (the OpenLineage
identity), served by the read API, **rendered in the UI**, sent to a third-party
catalog inside a query string, and logged in plaintext.

So these three helpers exist to make that class of bug structurally hard:

- `uri_password` — does this URI carry a password at all? (the create/update guard)
- `strip_uri_credentials` — the safe, *stable* form to persist and to show. Stable
  matters: a namespace derived from a credential-bearing URI **changes when the
  password is rotated**, silently forking the asset into a new identity and
  orphaning its lineage/incidents.
- `inject_uri_password` — put the credential back, at the last possible moment,
  from the SecretStore, only for the live connection.

The username is deliberately **kept**: it's an identifier, not a credential, and
it's part of what makes the URI a stable identity.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from urllib.parse import quote, urlsplit, urlunsplit


def uri_password(uri: str) -> str | None:
    """The password embedded in ``uri``'s userinfo, or ``None``.

    Returns ``None`` for a URI with no userinfo *and* for one carrying only a
    username (`scheme://user@host`) — a bare username is not a credential.
    """
    try:
        parts = urlsplit(uri)
    except ValueError:
        return None
    return parts.password or None


def strip_uri_credentials(uri: str) -> str:
    """``uri`` with any userinfo **password** removed, username preserved.

    `REDACTED` → `postgresql://u@h:5432/db?x=1`

    Non-URI / unparseable input is returned unchanged: this is used on config that
    may legitimately not be a URI at all (an Iceberg `catalog_uri` can be a bare
    `thrift://host` or even a path), so it must never mangle or raise.
    """
    try:
        parts = urlsplit(uri)
    except ValueError:
        return uri
    if not parts.password:
        return uri

    host = parts.hostname or ""
    if parts.port:
        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
    netloc = f"{parts.username}@{host}" if parts.username else host
    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))


def inject_uri_password(uri: str, password: str) -> str:
    """``uri`` with ``password`` set on its userinfo (the username must already be there).

    The inverse of `strip_uri_credentials`, applied at catalog-load time from the
    SecretStore. The password is **percent-encoded** so a credential containing
    `@`, `:` or `/` can't break out of the userinfo field and silently repoint the
    URI at another host — a URI-injection this function must not enable.

    Returns ``uri`` unchanged when it has no username to attach a password to, or
    when it already carries one (an explicit password in config wins, so this can
    never silently override an operator's intent).
    """
    try:
        parts = urlsplit(uri)
    except ValueError:
        return uri
    if not parts.username or parts.password:
        return uri

    host = parts.hostname or ""
    if parts.port:
        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
    netloc = f"{quote(parts.username, safe='')}:{quote(password, safe='')}@{host}"
    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))

import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py"); s = p.read_text()

# --- config: second secret slot + password guard ---
old_doc = '''    ``secret_property`` names the single ``load_catalog`` property the connection's
    secret fills — the only place the credential lands.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    catalog_name: str = "default"
    catalog_type: IcebergCatalogType
    catalog_uri: str | None = None
    warehouse: str | None = None
    properties: dict[str, str] = {}
    secret_property: str | None = None

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _uri_present(self) -> IcebergConfig:
        if self.catalog_type in _URI_REQUIRED and not self.catalog_uri:
            raise ValueError(f"catalog_uri is required for a {self.catalog_type!r} catalog")
        return self
'''
new_doc = '''    ``secret_property`` names the single ``load_catalog`` property the connection's
    secret fills — typically the *storage* credential (e.g. ``adls.account-key``).

    **A SQL catalog needs a SECOND credential** (the catalog DB password), and
    ``pyiceberg`` only accepts it inside the SQLAlchemy ``uri``. Putting it there in
    config is what caused #754/#826: `config` is non-secret, so the password was
    persisted, copied into the asset's OpenLineage namespace, served by the read API,
    **rendered in the UI**, and sent to third-party catalogs in a query string.

    So ``catalog_uri`` must ship **credential-less** (username is fine — that's an
    identifier, not a credential) and ``catalog_secret_name`` names a SecretStore
    entry holding the password. The caller resolves it and hands it in; it is injected
    into the URI's userinfo at catalog-load time and never persisted. A password left
    inline in ``catalog_uri`` is rejected outright by the validator below.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    catalog_name: str = "default"
    catalog_type: IcebergCatalogType
    catalog_uri: str | None = None
    warehouse: str | None = None
    properties: dict[str, str] = {}
    secret_property: str | None = None
    # A SecretStore *key name*, not a credential — safe to keep in non-secret config
    # (same idiom as the `*_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME` settings).
    catalog_secret_name: str | None = None

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _uri_present(self) -> IcebergConfig:
        if self.catalog_type in _URI_REQUIRED and not self.catalog_uri:
            raise ValueError(f"catalog_uri is required for a {self.catalog_type!r} catalog")
        return self

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _uri_carries_no_password(self) -> IcebergConfig:
        """Reject a password smuggled into `catalog_uri` (#754 AC2).

        `config` is NOT a secret: it is persisted in plaintext JSONB, returned by the
        read API, and used to derive the asset's OpenLineage identity. A credential in
        here leaks by construction, so refuse it at the door rather than redacting it
        forever after — and point the author at the slot that does the right thing.
        """
        if self.catalog_uri and uri_password(self.catalog_uri):
            raise ValueError(
                "catalog_uri must not embed a password (config is stored and returned "
                "in plaintext, and becomes the asset's lineage identity). Put the "
                "catalog credential in the secret store and name it via "
                "'catalog_secret_name'; keep the username in the URI."
            )
        return self
'''
assert old_doc in s
s = s.replace(old_doc, new_doc, 1)

# --- catalog_properties: inject the catalog secret into the URI ---
old_props = '''    def catalog_properties(self, secret: str | None) -> dict[str, str]:'''
new_props = '''    def catalog_properties(
        self, secret: str | None, catalog_secret: str | None = None
    ) -> dict[str, str]:'''
assert old_props in s
s = s.replace(old_props, new_props, 1)

old_uri = '''        props: dict[str, str] = dict(self.properties)
        props["type"] = self.catalog_type
        if self.catalog_uri:
            props["uri"] = self.catalog_uri'''
new_uri = '''        props: dict[str, str] = dict(self.properties)
        props["type"] = self.catalog_type
        if self.catalog_uri:
            # The catalog credential is re-attached HERE and nowhere else — the last
            # possible moment, in memory, for this one load. `catalog_uri` itself
            # stays credential-less at rest (#754/#826).
            props["uri"] = (
                inject_uri_password(self.catalog_uri, catalog_secret)
                if catalog_secret
                else self.catalog_uri
            )'''
assert old_uri in s
s = s.replace(old_uri, new_uri, 1)

s = s.replace(
    "from backend.app.core.errors import DataQError",
    "from backend.app.core.errors import DataQError\nfrom backend.app.core.uri_credentials import inject_uri_password, uri_password",
    1,
)
p.write_text(s)
print("iceberg config patched")

def load_iceberg_table(
    config: IcebergConfig,
    secret: str | None,
    identifier: str,
    catalog_secret: str | None = None,
) -> Any:
    """Load an Iceberg table by its ``namespace.table`` identifier (the live seam).

    The single catalog-load + ``load_table`` round-trip shared by the check/monitor
    runner and the column profiler (#721), so the two can't drift on how a
    connection's config + optional secret map to a ``pyiceberg`` catalog.
    ``pyiceberg`` is imported lazily (per this module's idiom) so the dependency
    only loads on a live Iceberg path.
    """
    from pyiceberg.catalog import load_catalog

    catalog: Any = load_catalog(config.catalog_name, **config.catalog_properties(secret))
    return catalog.load_table(identifier)

import pathlib
# base protocol
p = pathlib.Path("backend/app/datasources/base.py"); s = p.read_text()
s = s.replace(
"""    `validate_config` parses + validates a stored/incoming config (raising
    pydantic ``ValidationError`` on bad input) and returns the normalised model.
    `test` resolves connectivity against the live datasource using the config +
    its secret, raising on failure. Adapters never touch the DB or SecretStore —
    the caller resolves the secret and hands it in.
    \"\"\"

    def validate_config(self, raw: dict[str, Any]) -> BaseModel: ...

    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str) -> None: ...""",
"""    `validate_config` parses + validates a stored/incoming config (raising
    pydantic ``ValidationError`` on bad input) and returns the normalised model.
    `test` resolves connectivity against the live datasource using the config +
    its secret, raising on failure. Adapters never touch the DB or SecretStore —
    the caller resolves the secret and hands it in.

    Some types need MORE than one credential (an Iceberg SQL catalog needs the
    storage key *and* the catalog DB password). Rather than smuggle the second one
    into non-secret `config` — the #754/#826 bug — the caller resolves every extra
    secret named in config and passes them as keyword arguments. Adapters that need
    none simply ignore them (`**_`), so the seam's "caller resolves secrets"
    invariant holds for one credential or five.
    \"\"\"

    def validate_config(self, raw: dict[str, Any]) -> BaseModel: ...

    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str, **extra_secrets: Any) -> None: ...""", 1)
p.write_text(s)

# other adapters: accept + ignore extras
for f in ["adls.py","s3.py","unity_catalog.py","snowflake.py"]:
    p = pathlib.Path(f"backend/app/datasources/{f}"); s = p.read_text()
    s = s.replace("    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str) -> None:",
                  "    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str, **_: Any) -> None:", 1)
    p.write_text(s)
    print("patched", f)

def redact_config_uris(config: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:

    }

# add the generic resolver near the top-level helpers
anchor = "def "
helper = '''

def _extra_secrets(config: Mapping[str, Any], secret_store: SecretStore) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Resolve every *additional* credential a connection's config names, by convention.

    Some types need more than one credential (an Iceberg SQL catalog: the storage key
    AND the catalog DB password). Rather than smuggle the second into non-secret
    `config` — the #754/#826 bug — config holds only the SecretStore **key name**, in a
    field suffixed ``_secret_name``, and the caller (here) resolves it. `foo_secret_name`
    → the adapter receives ``foo_secret=<value>``.

    Generic on purpose: no branching on `connection.type`, so the seam keeps its "the
    caller resolves secrets, adapters never touch the store" invariant (ADR 0011) no
    matter how many credentials a future type needs. A named-but-missing secret is left
    out rather than raising, so `test()` surfaces it as a connectivity failure with the
    adapter's own message instead of a 500.
    """
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for key, value in config.items():
        if not key.endswith("_secret_name") or not isinstance(value, str) or not value:
            continue
        try:
            out[key.removesuffix("_name")] = secret_store.get(value)
        except SecretNotFoundError:
            log.warning("connection_extra_secret_missing", secret_field=key)
    return out

'''
idx = s.index("\ndef ", s.index("log = get_logger"))
s = s[:idx] + helper + s[idx:]
p.write_text(s)

def _resolve_iceberg(config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]) -> AssetIdentity:
    catalog_uri = config.get("catalog_uri")
    # NEVER let a credential into the identity (#826). The namespace is persisted on
    # `assets`, returned by the read API, RENDERED IN THE UI, and shipped to catalogs
    # inside a lineage query string — a password in here leaks by construction.
    # `IcebergConfig` now refuses to store one, but strip defensively anyway: legacy
    # rows exist, and this is the last gate before the value becomes an identity.
    #
    # Stripping also makes the identity STABLE: derived from a credential-bearing URI,
    # the namespace would silently CHANGE when the password is rotated — forking the
    # asset into a new row and orphaning its lineage and incidents.
    namespace = (
        strip_uri_credentials(catalog_uri.strip())
        if isinstance(catalog_uri, str) and catalog_uri.strip()
        else "file"
    )

input.content:
"""Strip URI credentials out of assets.namespace and connections.config (#754, #826).

Revision ID: c9d0e1f2a3b4
Revises: b7c8d9e0f1a2
Create Date: 2026-07-12

The Iceberg SQL-catalog password was carried inline in `connections.config.catalog_uri`
(the connection type had one secret slot, taken by the storage key), and
`asset_identity._resolve_iceberg` copied that URI **verbatim** into `assets.namespace`.
The credential was therefore persisted in two plaintext columns, served by the read API,
rendered in the UI, shipped to catalogs inside lineage query strings, and logged.

The code no longer produces this (the config now refuses a password in `catalog_uri`,
and the namespace is derived credential-free). This migration repairs the rows already
written.

**`assets.namespace` is UPDATED IN PLACE — deliberately.** The namespace is half of the
asset's identity, so writing a *new* credential-free row instead would fork the asset:
the old row (and every suite/run/lineage_edge/incident pointing at its id) would be
orphaned, and the UI would show a duplicate. Rewriting the existing row keeps `assets.id`
stable, so every foreign key follows automatically.

Uniqueness: `uq_assets_namespace_name` could in principle be violated if a credential-free
twin of a poisoned row already exists (e.g. someone re-created the connection correctly).
We therefore fold rather than collide — see `_merge_or_rewrite`.

**Down-migration cannot restore the credential, and must not.** Reversing this would mean
re-inserting a password into a plaintext column; the downgrade is intentionally a no-op
(the credential-free namespace is valid input to every reader), so a rollback is safe but
does not resurrect the leak.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from urllib.parse import urlsplit, urlunsplit

import sqlalchemy as sa
from alembic import op

revision = "c9d0e1f2a3b4"
down_revision = "b7c8d9e0f1a2"
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None


def _strip(uri: str) -> str:
    """`REDACTED` → `scheme://user@host/db` (username kept).

    Duplicated from `core.uri_credentials.strip_uri_credentials` on purpose: a migration
    must be pinned to the schema/logic of its own moment and keep working even if the
    application helper is later renamed or changed.
    """
    try:
        parts = urlsplit(uri)
    except ValueError:
        return uri
    if not parts.password:
        return uri
    host = parts.hostname or ""
    if parts.port:
        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
    netloc = f"{parts.username}@{host}" if parts.username else host
    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))


def _merge_or_rewrite(conn: sa.Connection, asset_id: str, old_ns: str, new_ns: str, name: str):
    """Point the asset at its credential-free namespace, folding onto a twin if one exists."""
    twin = conn.execute(
        sa.text(
            "SELECT id FROM assets WHERE namespace = :ns AND name = :name AND id <> :id"
        ),
        {"ns": new_ns, "name": name, "id": asset_id},
    ).scalar()
    if twin is None:
        conn.execute(
            sa.text("UPDATE assets SET namespace = :ns WHERE id = :id"),
            {"ns": new_ns, "id": asset_id},
        )
        return

    # A credential-free twin already exists: re-point everything that referenced the
    # poisoned row at it, then drop the poisoned row. Never leave dangling FKs.
    for table, col in (
        ("suites", "asset_id"),
        ("runs", "asset_id"),
        ("incidents", "asset_id"),
        ("lineage_edges", "upstream_asset_id"),
        ("lineage_edges", "downstream_asset_id"),
    ):
        conn.execute(
            sa.text(f"UPDATE {table} SET {col} = :twin WHERE {col} = :old"),  # noqa: S608
            {"twin": twin, "old": asset_id},
        )
    conn.execute(sa.text("DELETE FROM assets WHERE id = :id"), {"id": asset_id})


def upgrade() -> None:
    conn = op.get_bind()

    # 1) assets.namespace — the identity that reached the UI.
    rows = conn.execute(
        sa.text("SELECT id, namespace, name FROM assets WHERE namespace LIKE '%:%@%'")
    ).fetchall()
    for asset_id, namespace, name in rows:
        stripped = _strip(namespace)
        if stripped != namespace:
            _merge_or_rewrite(conn, asset_id, namespace, stripped, name)

    # 2) connections.config — any URI-shaped value carrying a password, any type.
    #    Generic, so a non-Iceberg type that ever did the same thing is caught too.
    conns = conn.execute(sa.text("SELECT id, config FROM connections")).fetchall()
    for conn_id, config in conns:
        if not isinstance(config, dict):
            continue
        cleaned = {
            k: (_strip(v) if isinstance(v, str) and "://" in v and "@" in v else v)
            for k, v in config.items()
        }
        if cleaned != config:
            conn.execute(
                sa.text("UPDATE connections SET config = :cfg WHERE id = :id"),
                {"cfg": sa.text("").bindparams()  # placeholder replaced below
                 if False else __import__("json").dumps(cleaned), "id": conn_id},
            )


def downgrade() -> None:
    """No-op — see the module docstring.

    Reversing this would mean writing a password back into a plaintext column. The
    credential-free namespace/config is valid input for every reader, so a rollback is
    safe as-is; the leak is simply not resurrected.
    """

# ── credentials must never reach the asset identity (#826) ───────────────────


def test_iceberg_namespace_never_carries_the_catalog_password() -> None:
    """The namespace is persisted, served by the API, RENDERED IN THE UI and shipped to
    catalogs inside a lineage query string. A credential in it leaks by construction."""
    identity = resolve_asset_identity(
        "iceberg",
        {
            "catalog_uri": (
                "REDACTED"
                "iceberg_catalog?sslmode=require"
            )
        },
        {"namespace": "retail", "table": "purchase_orders"},
    )
    assert "s3cr3t" not in identity.namespace
    assert identity.namespace == (
        "postgresql+psycopg2://airflowadmin@pg.example.com:5432/iceberg_catalog?sslmode=require"
    )
    assert identity.name == "retail.purchase_orders"


def test_iceberg_identity_is_stable_across_a_password_rotation() -> None:
    """Rotating the catalog password must NOT fork the asset into a new identity.

    A namespace derived from a credential-bearing URI changes when the password does —
    silently creating a second `assets` row and orphaning the original's lineage and
    incidents. Stripping the credential makes the identity depend only on *where* the
    catalog is, which is what an identity should mean.
    """
    target = {"namespace": "retail", "table": "purchase_orders"}
    before = resolve_asset_identity(
        "iceberg", {"catalog_uri": "REDACTED"}, target
    )
    after = resolve_asset_identity(
        "iceberg", {"catalog_uri": "REDACTED"}, target
    )
    assert before == after
EOF

# ── the catalog credential must live in the SecretStore, not in config (#754/#826) ──


class TestCatalogCredential:
    def test_a_password_in_catalog_uri_is_rejected_outright(self) -> None:
        """`config` is stored and returned in plaintext AND becomes the asset's lineage
        identity — so refuse the credential at the door rather than redacting forever."""
        with pytest.raises(ValidationError) as exc:
            IcebergConfig(
                catalog_type="sql",
                catalog_uri="REDACTED",
            )
        assert "catalog_secret_name" in str(exc.value)

    def test_a_credential_less_uri_with_a_username_is_fine(self) -> None:
        cfg = IcebergConfig(
            catalog_type="sql",
            catalog_uri="postgresql+psycopg2://u@h:5432/cat?sslmode=require",
            catalog_secret_name="kv-iceberg-catalog",
        )
        assert cfg.catalog_secret_name == "kv-iceberg-catalog"

    def test_the_password_is_attached_only_at_catalog_load(self) -> None:
        cfg = IcebergConfig(
            catalog_type="sql",
            catalog_uri="postgresql+psycopg2://u@h:5432/cat",
            catalog_secret_name="kv-cat",
        )
        # At rest: no credential anywhere in the config.
        assert "s3cr3t" not in cfg.model_dump_json()
        # At load: the resolved secret is injected into the URI pyiceberg needs.
        props = cfg.catalog_properties(None, "s3cr3t")
        assert props["uri"] == "REDACTED"
        # …and without the secret it stays credential-less rather than half-built.
        assert cfg.catalog_properties(None)["uri"] == "postgresql+psycopg2://u@h:5432/cat"

    def test_a_hostile_password_cannot_repoint_the_uri(self) -> None:
        cfg = IcebergConfig(catalog_type="sql", catalog_uri="postgresql://u@real-host:5432/cat")
        uri = cfg.catalog_properties(None, "pw@evil-host/")
        assert "@real-host:5432/cat" in uri["uri"]
        assert "evil-host" not in uri["uri"].split("@")[-1]
EOF

result.output:
### 3. `iceberg` connection = self-contained (Option A), **not** a reference to an ADLS/S3 connection

An Iceberg connection needs two things the flat-file connection lacks: a **catalog pointer** (where the metadata lives) and **storage credentials** (to fetch data files). Two models were considered:

| | **A. Self-contained (chosen)** | **B. Two-connection reference** |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | `iceberg` connection carries catalog config **+ its own** storage credential (one `config` + one `secret_ref`) | `iceberg` connection carries catalog config **+ a reference** to an existing `adls_gen2`/`s3` connection for storage |
| Fits current code | **Yes** — drops into `build_check_runner` with no new plumbing; matches the "one `secret_ref` per connection" invariant every adapter already holds | **No** — the model has no connection→connection FK; runner builders take a single config/secret |
| Lifecycle | Independent — deleting the flat-file connection can't strand the Iceberg one | Coupled — shared credential + the cross-connection FK entangle lifecycles |
| Cost | Storage credential is duplicated if the user also has that ADLS/S3 connection | Cleaner credential reuse |

**Chosen: Option A.** It ships inside the existing `ConnectionAdapter`/`CheckRunner` seams with zero new cross-connection machinery, and it keeps lifecycles independent — which matches the real usage pattern: a user may register an ADLS path for **flat files and Iceberg tables both**, then later delete the flat-file connection and use that storage **only for Iceberg going forward**. Under Option A that deletion is clean — the `iceberg` connection owns its own storage credential and is unaffected, and the existing cascade-delete on the connection FK (ADR 0020) removes only the flat-file connection's own suites/checks. Under Option B the same action would either strand the Iceberg connection's storage credential or cascade into it. Credential duplication is the accepted cost.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
F=$(ls docs/adr/0030-*.md)
python3 - "$F" <<'PY'
import pathlib, sys
p = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[1]); s = p.read_text()
anchor = "**Chosen: Option A.** It ships inside the existing `ConnectionAdapter`/`CheckRunner` seams"
i = s.index(anchor)
end = s.index("\n\n", s.index("Credential duplication is the accepted cost."))
addition = """

#### 3a. Amendment (2026-07-12): "one `secret_ref` per connection" was **wrong for a SQL catalog** — #754 / #826

Option A assumed one credential per connection. A **SQL catalog** needs *two*: the storage key **and** the catalog DB password. With the single `secret_ref` slot taken by the storage key, the catalog password had nowhere legitimate to go — so it was smuggled inline into `config.catalog_uri`.

`config` is **not** a secret. It is persisted in plaintext JSONB, returned verbatim by `GET /connections`, and — because ADR 0034 derives the Iceberg asset's OpenLineage namespace from `catalog_uri` — it was copied into `assets.namespace`, **rendered as label text in the UI** (including as a tree-folder name, beside a Copy button), shipped to third-party catalogs inside a lineage query string, and logged in plaintext. A credential in `config` leaks *by construction*.

**Amended decision.** A connection may name **additional** secrets in config, by convention:

- `config.<x>_secret_name` holds a **SecretStore key name** (an identifier, not a credential — safe in plaintext config). Iceberg uses `catalog_secret_name`.
- `catalog_uri` must ship **credential-less** (username kept — it is an identifier). `IcebergConfig` now **rejects** a URI carrying a password, pointing the author at the secret slot.
- The **caller** resolves every `*_secret_name` and passes the values to the adapter as keyword arguments; the credential is injected into the URI at catalog-load time and never persisted. This preserves the ADR 0011 seam invariant — *adapters never touch the SecretStore* — while scaling past one credential.
- The namespace is derived from the **stripped** URI, which additionally makes the asset identity **stable across a password rotation** (previously, rotating the password silently forked the asset into a new row, orphaning its lineage and incidents).

Migration `c9d0e1f2a3b4` repairs rows written before the guard, updating `assets.namespace` **in place** so `assets.id` — and every FK to it — survives.
"""
s = s[:end] + addition + s[end:]
p.write_text(s)
print("ADR 0030 amended")
PY
grep -n "3a. Amendment" docs/adr/0030-*.md

content.text: Now commit and open the PR.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(security): keep catalog credentials out of config, the asset identity, and the API

The Iceberg SQL-catalog password was rendered as plaintext label text in the UI —
on the dashboard, as the NAME OF A TREE FOLDER on /assets, and on the asset detail
next to a Copy button. It was also readable via GET /connections, egressed to a
third-party catalog inside a lineage query string, and logged in the clear.

Root cause (ADR 0030 §3's "one secret_ref per connection" assumption): a SQL
catalog needs TWO credentials — the storage key and the catalog DB password — and
the single secret slot was taken by the storage key, so the password was smuggled
inline into config.catalog_uri. `config` is NOT a secret: it is persisted in
plaintext, returned by the read API, and (ADR 0034) copied verbatim into
assets.namespace, which the UI renders. A credential there leaks by construction.

Fixed at the root, in four layers:

- Iceberg gains a SECOND credential slot: `catalog_secret_name` holds a SecretStore
  KEY NAME (an identifier, safe in config); `catalog_uri` ships credential-less
  (username kept), and the password is injected into the URI at catalog-load time
  only. The caller resolves every `*_secret_name` generically and passes them as
  kwargs, so the ADR 0011 invariant — adapters never touch the SecretStore — holds
  for one credential or five, with no branching on connection.type.
- A validator REJECTS a password embedded in catalog_uri outright, pointing the
  author at the secret slot. Refuse it at the door rather than redact it forever.
- asset_identity strips URI credentials, so the OpenLineage namespace can never
  carry one. This also makes the identity STABLE across a password rotation —
  previously, rotating the password silently forked the asset into a new row and
  orphaned its lineage and incidents.
- The read API scrubs any URI password from any config value of any connection
  type (defence in depth, for rows written before the guard).

Injection percent-encodes the password, so one containing `@ : /` cannot break out
of the userinfo and repoint the URI at another host.

Migration c9d0e1f2a3b4 repairs already-poisoned rows, updating assets.namespace
IN PLACE so assets.id — and every FK to it — survives; it folds onto a
credential-free twin if one exists rather than colliding on the unique index.

Fixes #754

gh pr create --title "fix(security): keep catalog credentials out of config, the asset identity, and the API (#826, #754)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fixes #826. Fixes #754.

## The leak

A live Postgres password was **rendered as plaintext label text in the UI** — on the dashboard, as the *name of a tree folder* on `/assets`, and on the asset detail **next to a Copy button**. It was also readable via `GET /connections`, egressed to a third-party catalog **inside a lineage query string**, and logged in the clear.

## Root cause

ADR 0030 §3 assumed *one `secret_ref` per connection*. A **SQL catalog needs two** credentials — the storage key **and** the catalog DB password — and the single slot was taken by the storage key. So the password got smuggled inline into `config.catalog_uri`.

`config` is **not a secret**: it's persisted in plaintext JSONB, returned verbatim by the read API, and (ADR 0034) copied **verbatim into `assets.namespace`**, which the UI renders. A credential in `config` leaks *by construction* — which is why redaction alone would have been a band-aid.

## Fixed at the root, in four layers

| Layer | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| **New connections** | password accepted in `catalog_uri` | **rejected** by a validator, pointing at the secret slot |
| **At rest** | password in plaintext `config` + `assets.namespace` | `catalog_uri` credential-less; password in the SecretStore, named by `catalog_secret_name` |
| **Read API / UI** | returned + rendered verbatim | any URI password scrubbed from any config value, any type |
| **Live catalog load** | — | password injected into the URI **in memory, for that one load** |

The caller resolves every `*_secret_name` **generically** and passes them as kwargs, so the ADR 0011 seam invariant — *adapters never touch the SecretStore* — holds for one credential or five, **with no branching on `connection.type`**.

Injection **percent-encodes** the password, so one containing `@ : /` can't break out of the userinfo and repoint the URI at another host (test asserts the real host survives).

## Two bugs this also fixes

- **Identity is now stable across a password rotation.** Previously the namespace was derived from the credential-bearing URI, so rotating the password **silently forked the asset into a new row**, orphaning its lineage and incidents.
- The migration folds onto a credential-free twin if one exists, rather than colliding on `uq_assets_namespace_name`.

## Migration `c9d0e1f2a3b4`
Repairs already-poisoned rows. `assets.namespace` is updated **IN PLACE** — deliberately: writing a new row would fork the asset and orphan every suite/run/lineage_edge/incident pointing at its id. Tested **up → down → up** against real Postgres with a seeded poisoned row (`LEAKEDPW` gone, username preserved, zero leaking namespaces, FKs intact). Downgrade is intentionally a no-op: reversing it would mean writing a password back into a plaintext column.

## Testing
- New `core/uri_credentials` suite: strip/inject round-trip, idempotence, non-DSN URIs passed through untouched, and the URI-injection guard.
- Iceberg: guard rejects an inline password; credential-less URI + secret name accepted; password attached **only** at load; hostile password can't repoint the URI.
- `asset_identity`: namespace never carries the password; **identity unchanged across a rotation**.
- Full gate: black, ruff, **bandit**, mypy (app+tests+scripts), **2,119 backend tests, 98.19%**.

ADR 0030 amended (§3a) with the outcome — #754's AC4.

**Ops follow-up (not in this PR):** the exposed Postgres password must be rotated and the `harness-iceberg` connection re-pointed at a SecretStore entry. Doing that next.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

+"""Strip URI credentials out of assets.namespace and connections.config (#754, #826).
+
+Revision ID: c9d0e1f2a3b4
+Revises: b7c8d9e0f1a2
+Create Date: 2026-07-12
+
+The Iceberg SQL-catalog password was carried inline in `connections.config.catalog_uri`
+(the connection type had one secret slot, taken by the storage key), and
+`asset_identity._resolve_iceberg` copied that URI **verbatim** into `assets.namespace`.
+The credential was therefore persisted in two plaintext columns, served by the read API,
+rendered in the UI, shipped to catalogs inside lineage query strings, and logged.
+
+The code no longer produces this (the config now refuses a password in `catalog_uri`,
+and the namespace is derived credential-free). This migration repairs the rows already
+written.
+
+**`assets.namespace` is UPDATED IN PLACE — deliberately.** The namespace is half of the
+asset's identity, so writing a *new* credential-free row instead would fork the asset:
+the old row (and every suite/run/lineage_edge/incident pointing at its id) would be
+orphaned, and the UI would show a duplicate. Rewriting the existing row keeps `assets.id`
+stable, so every foreign key follows automatically.
+
+Uniqueness: `uq_assets_namespace_name` could in principle be violated if a credential-free
+twin of a poisoned row already exists (e.g. someone re-created the connection correctly).
+We therefore fold rather than collide — see `_merge_or_rewrite`.
+
+**Down-migration cannot restore the credential, and must not.** Reversing this would mean
+re-inserting a password into a plaintext column; the downgrade is intentionally a no-op
+(the credential-free namespace is valid input to every reader), so a rollback is safe but
+does not resurrect the leak.
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import json
+from urllib.parse import urlsplit, urlunsplit
+
+import sqlalchemy as sa
+
+from alembic import op
+
+revision = "c9d0e1f2a3b4"
+down_revision = "b7c8d9e0f1a2"
+branch_labels = None
+depends_on = None
+
+
+def _strip(uri: str) -> str:
+    """`REDACTED` → `scheme://user@host/db` (username kept).
+
+    Duplicated from `core.uri_credentials.strip_uri_credentials` on purpose: a migration
+    must be pinned to the schema/logic of its own moment and keep working even if the
+    application helper is later renamed or changed.
+    """
+    try:
+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
+    except ValueError:
+        return uri
+    if not parts.password:
+        return uri
+    host = parts.hostname or ""
+    if parts.port:
+        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
+    netloc = f"{parts.username}@{host}" if parts.username else host
+    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))
+
+
+def _merge_or_rewrite(conn: sa.Connection, asset_id: str, new_ns: str, name: str) -> None:
+    """Point the asset at its credential-free namespace, folding onto a twin if one exists."""
+    twin = conn.execute(
+        sa.text("SELECT id FROM assets WHERE namespace = :ns AND name = :name AND id <> :id"),
+        {"ns": new_ns, "name": name, "id": asset_id},
+    ).scalar()
+    if twin is None:
+        conn.execute(
+            sa.text("UPDATE assets SET namespace = :ns WHERE id = :id"),
+            {"ns": new_ns, "id": asset_id},
+        )
+        return
+
+    # A credential-free twin already exists: re-point everything that referenced the
+    # poisoned row at it, then drop the poisoned row. Never leave dangling FKs.
+    for table, col in (
+        ("suites", "asset_id"),
+        ("runs", "asset_id"),
+        ("incidents", "asset_id"),
+        ("lineage_edges", "upstream_asset_id"),
+        ("lineage_edges", "downstream_asset_id"),
+    ):
+        conn.execute(
+            sa.text(f"UPDATE {table} SET {col} = :twin WHERE {col} = :old"),  # noqa: S608
+            {"twin": twin, "old": asset_id},
+        )
+    conn.execute(sa.text("DELETE FROM assets WHERE id = :id"), {"id": asset_id})
+
+

+    conn = op.get_bind()

+    rows = conn.execute(

+    ).fetchall()
+    for asset_id, namespace, name in rows:

+        if stripped != namespace:

+    # 2) connections.config — any URI-shaped value carrying a password, any type.
+    #    Generic, so a non-Iceberg type that ever did the same thing is caught too.
+    #    The credential itself is NOT discarded blindly: an operator must re-point the
+    #    connection at a SecretStore entry (`*_secret_name`) — see the issue. This
+    #    migration only stops the plaintext copy from sitting in the DB and the API.
+    conns = conn.execute(sa.text("SELECT id, config FROM connections")).fetchall()

+            continue

+            k: (_strip(v) if isinstance(v, str) and "://" in v and "@" in v else v)

+        if cleaned != config:
+            conn.execute(

+                {"cfg": json.dumps(cleaned), "id": conn_id},

+    """No-op — see the module docstring.

+    Reversing this would mean writing a password back into a plaintext column. The
+    credential-free namespace/config is valid input for every reader, so a rollback is
+    safe as-is; the leak is simply not resurrected.
+    """

+++ b/backend/app/core/uri_credentials.py
@@ -0,0 +1,108 @@
+"""URI userinfo handling — keep credentials OUT of connection URIs (#754, #826).
+
+A DSN-shaped URI (`REDACTED`) can smuggle a
+password through any field that was only ever meant to hold a *location*. That is
+exactly what happened to the Iceberg SQL-catalog `catalog_uri`: the connection type
+has one secret slot (used by the storage key), so the catalog DB password ended up
+inline in the URI — a *non-secret* config field. From there it was persisted to
+`connections.config`, copied verbatim into `assets.namespace` (the OpenLineage
+identity), served by the read API, **rendered in the UI**, sent to a third-party
+catalog inside a query string, and logged in plaintext.
+
+So these three helpers exist to make that class of bug structurally hard:
+
+- `uri_password` — does this URI carry a password at all? (the create/update guard)
+- `strip_uri_credentials` — the safe, *stable* form to persist and to show. Stable
+  matters: a namespace derived from a credential-bearing URI **changes when the
+  password is rotated**, silently forking the asset into a new identity and
+  orphaning its lineage/incidents.
+- `inject_uri_password` — put the credential back, at the last possible moment,
+  from the SecretStore, only for the live connection.
+
+The username is deliberately **kept**: it's an identifier, not a credential, and
+it's part of what makes the URI a stable identity.
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+from collections.abc import Mapping
+from typing import Any
+from urllib.parse import quote, urlsplit, urlunsplit
+
+
+def uri_password(uri: str) -> str | None:
+    """The password embedded in ``uri``'s userinfo, or ``None``.
+
+    Returns ``None`` for a URI with no userinfo *and* for one carrying only a
+    username (`scheme://user@host`) — a bare username is not a credential.
+    """
+    try:
+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
+    except ValueError:
+        return None
+    return parts.password or None
+
+
+def strip_uri_credentials(uri: str) -> str:
+    """``uri`` with any userinfo **password** removed, username preserved.
+
+    `REDACTED` → `postgresql://u@h:5432/db?x=1`
+
+    Non-URI / unparseable input is returned unchanged: this is used on config that
+    may legitimately not be a URI at all (an Iceberg `catalog_uri` can be a bare
+    `thrift://host` or even a path), so it must never mangle or raise.
+    """
+    try:
+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
+    except ValueError:
+        return uri
+    if not parts.password:
+        return uri
+
+    host = parts.hostname or ""
+    if parts.port:
+        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
+    netloc = f"{parts.username}@{host}" if parts.username else host
+    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))
+
+
+def inject_uri_password(uri: str, password: str) -> str:
+    """``uri`` with ``password`` set on its userinfo (the username must already be there).
+
+    The inverse of `strip_uri_credentials`, applied at catalog-load time from the
+    SecretStore. The password is **percent-encoded** so a credential containing
+    `@`, `:` or `/` can't break out of the userinfo field and silently repoint the
+    URI at another host — a URI-injection this function must not enable.
+
+    Returns ``uri`` unchanged when it has no username to attach a password to, or
+    when it already carries one (an explicit password in config wins, so this can
+    never silently override an operator's intent).
+    """
+    try:
+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
+    except ValueError:
+        return uri
+    if not parts.username or parts.password:
+        return uri
+
+    host = parts.hostname or ""
+    if parts.port:
+        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
+    netloc = f"{quote(parts.username, safe='')}:{quote(password, safe='')}@{host}"
+    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))
+
+
+def redact_config_uris(config: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
+    """A copy of ``config`` with any URI-embedded password stripped from string values.
+
+    **Defence in depth, not the fix.** The fix is refusing to store the credential at
+    all (`IcebergConfig` rejects a password in `catalog_uri`). But rows written before
+    that guard existed still carry one, and `config` is handed to the read API — so
+    anything leaving the service is scrubbed on the way out, generically, for every
+    connection type. No type-branching: a value is redacted iff it parses as a URI
+    that carries a password.
+    """
+    return {
+        k: (strip_uri_credentials(v) if isinstance(v, str) and uri_password(v) else v)
+        for k, v in config.items()
+    }

27	+"""Strip URI credentials out of assets.namespace and connections.config (#754, #826).
28	+
29	+Revision ID: c9d0e1f2a3b4
30	+Revises: b7c8d9e0f1a2
31	+Create Date: 2026-07-12
32	+
33	+The Iceberg SQL-catalog password was carried inline in `connections.config.catalog_uri`
34	+(the connection type had one secret slot, taken by the storage key), and
35	+`asset_identity._resolve_iceberg` copied that URI **verbatim** into `assets.namespace`.
36	+The credential was therefore persisted in two plaintext columns, served by the read API,
37	+rendered in the UI, shipped to catalogs inside lineage query strings, and logged.
38	+
39	+The code no longer produces this (the config now refuses a password in `catalog_uri`,
40	+and the namespace is derived credential-free). This migration repairs the rows already
41	+written.
42	+
43	+**`assets.namespace` is UPDATED IN PLACE — deliberately.** The namespace is half of the
44	+asset's identity, so writing a *new* credential-free row instead would fork the asset:
45	+the old row (and every suite/run/lineage_edge/incident pointing at its id) would be
46	+orphaned, and the UI would show a duplicate. Rewriting the existing row keeps `assets.id`
47	+stable, so every foreign key follows automatically.
48	+
49	+Uniqueness: `uq_assets_namespace_name` could in principle be violated if a credential-free
50	+twin of a poisoned row already exists (e.g. someone re-created the connection correctly).
51	+We therefore fold rather than collide — see `_merge_or_rewrite`.
52	+
53	+**Down-migration cannot restore the credential, and must not.** Reversing this would mean
54	+re-inserting a password into a plaintext column; the downgrade is intentionally a no-op
55	+(the credential-free namespace is valid input to every reader), so a rollback is safe but
56	+does not resurrect the leak.
57	+"""
58	+
59	+from __future__ import annotations
60	+
61	+import json
62	+from urllib.parse import urlsplit, urlunsplit
63	+
64	+import sqlalchemy as sa
65	+
66	+from alembic import op
67	+
68	+revision = "c9d0e1f2a3b4"
69	+down_revision = "b7c8d9e0f1a2"
70	+branch_labels = None
71	+depends_on = None
72	+
73	+
74	+def _strip(uri: str) -> str:
75	+    """`REDACTED` → `scheme://user@host/db` (username kept).
76	+
77	+    Duplicated from `core.uri_credentials.strip_uri_credentials` on purpose: a migration
78	+    must be pinned to the schema/logic of its own moment and keep working even if the
79	+    application helper is later renamed or changed.
80	+    """
81	+    try:
82	+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
83	+    except ValueError:
84	+        return uri
85	+    if not parts.password:
86	+        return uri
87	+    host = parts.hostname or ""
88	+    if parts.port:
89	+        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
90	+    netloc = f"{parts.username}@{host}" if parts.username else host
91	+    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))
92	+
93	+
94	+def _merge_or_rewrite(conn: sa.Connection, asset_id: str, new_ns: str, name: str) -> None:
95	+    """Point the asset at its credential-free namespace, folding onto a twin if one exists."""
96	+    twin = conn.execute(
97	+        sa.text("SELECT id FROM assets WHERE namespace = :ns AND name = :name AND id <> :id"),
98	+        {"ns": new_ns, "name": name, "id": asset_id},
99	+    ).scalar()
100	+    if twin is None:
101	+        conn.execute(
102	+            sa.text("UPDATE assets SET namespace = :ns WHERE id = :id"),
103	+            {"ns": new_ns, "id": asset_id},
104	+        )
105	+        return
106	+
107	+    # A credential-free twin already exists: re-point everything that referenced the
108	+    # poisoned row at it, then drop the poisoned row. Never leave dangling FKs.
109	+    for table, col in (
110	+        ("suites", "asset_id"),
111	+        ("runs", "asset_id"),
112	+        ("incidents", "asset_id"),
113	+        ("lineage_edges", "upstream_asset_id"),
114	+        ("lineage_edges", "downstream_asset_id"),
115	+    ):
116	+        conn.execute(
117	+            sa.text(f"UPDATE {table} SET {col} = :twin WHERE {col} = :old"),  # noqa: S608
118	+            {"twin": twin, "old": asset_id},
119	+        )
120	+    conn.execute(sa.text("DELETE FROM assets WHERE id = :id"), {"id": asset_id})
121	+
122	+
123	+def upgrade() -> None:
124	+    conn = op.get_bind()
125	+
126	+    # 1) assets.namespace — the identity that reached the UI.
127	+    rows = conn.execute(
128	+        sa.text("SELECT id, namespace, name FROM assets WHERE namespace LIKE '%:%@%'")
129	+    ).fetchall()
130	+    for asset_id, namespace, name in rows:
131	+        stripped = _strip(namespace)
132	+        if stripped != namespace:
133	+            _merge_or_rewrite(conn, asset_id, stripped, name)
134	+
135	+    # 2) connections.config — any URI-shaped value carrying a password, any type.
136	+    #    Generic, so a non-Iceberg type that ever did the same thing is caught too.
137	+    #    The credential itself is NOT discarded blindly: an operator must re-point the
138	+    #    connection at a SecretStore entry (`*_secret_name`) — see the issue. This
139	+    #    migration only stops the plaintext copy from sitting in the DB and the API.
140	+    conns = conn.execute(sa.text("SELECT id, config FROM connections")).fetchall()
141	+    for conn_id, config in conns:
142	+        if not isinstance(config, dict):
143	+            continue
144	+        cleaned = {
145	+            k: (_strip(v) if isinstance(v, str) and "://" in v and "@" in v else v)
146	+            for k, v in config.items()
147	+        }
148	+        if cleaned != config:
149	+            conn.execute(
150	+                sa.text("UPDATE connections SET config = CAST(:cfg AS jsonb) WHERE id = :id"),
151	+                {"cfg": json.dumps(cleaned), "id": conn_id},
152	+            )
153	+

155	+def downgrade() -> None:
156	+    """No-op — see the module docstring.
157	+
158	+    Reversing this would mean writing a password back into a plaintext column. The
159	+    credential-free namespace/config is valid input for every reader, so a rollback is
160	+    safe as-is; the leak is simply not resurrected.
161	+    """

208	new file mode 100644
209	index 00000000..a5ac0fba
210	--- /dev/null
211	+++ b/backend/app/core/uri_credentials.py
212	@@ -0,0 +1,108 @@
213	+"""URI userinfo handling — keep credentials OUT of connection URIs (#754, #826).
214	+
215	+A DSN-shaped URI (`REDACTED`) can smuggle a
216	+password through any field that was only ever meant to hold a *location*. That is
217	+exactly what happened to the Iceberg SQL-catalog `catalog_uri`: the connection type
218	+has one secret slot (used by the storage key), so the catalog DB password ended up
219	+inline in the URI — a *non-secret* config field. From there it was persisted to
220	+`connections.config`, copied verbatim into `assets.namespace` (the OpenLineage
221	+identity), served by the read API, **rendered in the UI**, sent to a third-party
222	+catalog inside a query string, and logged in plaintext.
223	+
224	+So these three helpers exist to make that class of bug structurally hard:
225	+
226	+- `uri_password` — does this URI carry a password at all? (the create/update guard)
227	+- `strip_uri_credentials` — the safe, *stable* form to persist and to show. Stable
228	+  matters: a namespace derived from a credential-bearing URI **changes when the
229	+  password is rotated**, silently forking the asset into a new identity and
230	+  orphaning its lineage/incidents.
231	+- `inject_uri_password` — put the credential back, at the last possible moment,
232	+  from the SecretStore, only for the live connection.
233	+
234	+The username is deliberately **kept**: it's an identifier, not a credential, and
235	+it's part of what makes the URI a stable identity.
236	+"""
237	+
238	+from __future__ import annotations
239	+
240	+from collections.abc import Mapping
241	+from typing import Any
242	+from urllib.parse import quote, urlsplit, urlunsplit
243	+
244	+
245	+def uri_password(uri: str) -> str | None:
246	+    """The password embedded in ``uri``'s userinfo, or ``None``.
247	+
248	+    Returns ``None`` for a URI with no userinfo *and* for one carrying only a
249	+    username (`scheme://user@host`) — a bare username is not a credential.
250	+    """
251	+    try:
252	+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
253	+    except ValueError:
254	+        return None
255	+    return parts.password or None
256	+
257	+
258	+def strip_uri_credentials(uri: str) -> str:
259	+    """``uri`` with any userinfo **password** removed, username preserved.
260	+
261	+    `REDACTED` → `postgresql://u@h:5432/db?x=1`
262	+
263	+    Non-URI / unparseable input is returned unchanged: this is used on config that
264	+    may legitimately not be a URI at all (an Iceberg `catalog_uri` can be a bare
265	+    `thrift://host` or even a path), so it must never mangle or raise.
266	+    """
267	+    try:
268	+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
269	+    except ValueError:
270	+        return uri
271	+    if not parts.password:
272	+        return uri
273	+
274	+    host = parts.hostname or ""
275	+    if parts.port:
276	+        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
277	+    netloc = f"{parts.username}@{host}" if parts.username else host
278	+    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))
279	+
280	+
281	+def inject_uri_password(uri: str, password: str) -> str:
282	+    """``uri`` with ``password`` set on its userinfo (the username must already be there).
283	+
284	+    The inverse of `strip_uri_credentials`, applied at catalog-load time from the
285	+    SecretStore. The password is **percent-encoded** so a credential containing
286	+    `@`, `:` or `/` can't break out of the userinfo field and silently repoint the
287	+    URI at another host — a URI-injection this function must not enable.
288	+
289	+    Returns ``uri`` unchanged when it has no username to attach a password to, or
290	+    when it already carries one (an explicit password in config wins, so this can
291	+    never silently override an operator's intent).
292	+    """
293	+    try:
294	+        parts = urlsplit(uri)
295	+    except ValueError:
296	+        return uri
297	+    if not parts.username or parts.password:
298	+        return uri
299	+
300	+    host = parts.hostname or ""
301	+    if parts.port:
302	+        host = f"{host}:{parts.port}"
303	+    netloc = f"{quote(parts.username, safe='')}:{quote(password, safe='')}@{host}"
304	+    return urlunsplit((parts.scheme, netloc, parts.path, parts.query, parts.fragment))
305	+
306	+
307	+def redact_config_uris(config: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
308	+    """A copy of ``config`` with any URI-embedded password stripped from string values.
309	+
310	+    **Defence in depth, not the fix.** The fix is refusing to store the credential at
311	+    all (`IcebergConfig` rejects a password in `catalog_uri`). But rows written before
312	+    that guard existed still carry one, and `config` is handed to the read API — so
313	+    anything leaving the service is scrubbed on the way out, generically, for every
314	+    connection type. No type-branching: a value is redacted iff it parses as a URI
315	+    that carries a password.
316	+    """

cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ

echo "=== dataset_reader (comparison, #792) ==="
grep -rn "iceberg\|load_iceberg\|catalog_secret" backend/app/services/dataset_reader.py | head -6

content.text: **Found a real bug in my own change.** The profiler (lines 721, 741) and the comparison `dataset_reader` call `load_iceberg_table`/`list_iceberg_columns` **without** the catalog secret. Since `catalog_uri` is now credential-less, those paths would connect with **no password** — silently breaking column-profiling and comparison checks on any SQL-catalog Iceberg connection. Only the check-runner path was wired. Let me fix all of them.

result.output:
def _read_iceberg_dataframe(
    connection: Connection, *, identifier: str, columns: list[str], secret_store: SecretStore
) -> Any:
    """Resolve an Iceberg connection's config + optional secret (exactly as
    `build_iceberg_runner` does — the credential is optional), load the table
    ONCE, validate the requested `columns` against its schema *before any scan*
    (raising the same `ProfileColumnNotFoundError` the post-scan defence-in-depth
    check in `_profile_columns` raises — same message/detail shape — so an
    all-or-partially-invalid column list 422s without ever reading data), then
    materialise the already-loaded table as a projected, sampled DataFrame.

    The live I/O seam (catalog load + scan), monkeypatched in tests — the
    Iceberg analogue of the flat-file profiler's `_read_dataframe`. Before this
    fold, an all-invalid column list fell through to `read_iceberg_dataframe`'s
    ``selected_fields=("*",)`` fallback, scanning every column before the 422
    (#721 code review); that fallback now only fires for the `columns=None`
    (list-every-column) case, never reachable from here."""
    config = IcebergConfig.model_validate(connection.config)
    secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref) if connection.secret_ref else None
    table = load_iceberg_table(config, secret, identifier)
    available = [field.name for field in table.schema().fields]
    missing = [c for c in columns if c not in available]
    if missing:
        raise ProfileColumnNotFoundError(
            "requested column(s) not in the target",
            detail={"missing": missing, "available": available[:50]},
        )
    return read_iceberg_dataframe(
        config, secret, identifier, columns=columns, limit=_SAMPLE_ROWS, table=table
    )


def _list_iceberg_columns(
    connection: Connection, *, identifier: str, secret_store: SecretStore
) -> list[str]:
    """Resolve config + optional secret and list the target's schema field names
    (metadata only, no data scan) — the Iceberg column-listing I/O seam."""
    config = IcebergConfig.model_validate(connection.config)
    secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref) if connection.secret_ref else None
    return iceberg_column_names(config, secret, identifier)

def _iceberg_read(
    connection: Connection, spec: DatasetSpec, max_rows: int, secret_store: SecretStore
) -> Any:
    if not spec.table:
        raise DatasetReadUnsupportedError(
            "iceberg comparison side needs a namespace.table identifier",
            detail={"spec": "table"},
        )
    cfg = IcebergConfig.model_validate(connection.config)
    # Credential optional, mirroring `build_iceberg_runner` (a local warehouse /
    # vended-credentials REST catalog has none).
    secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref) if connection.secret_ref else None
    table = load_iceberg_table(cfg, secret, spec.table)
    count = int(table.scan().count())  # snapshot metadata — no data files read
    if count > max_rows:
        raise _too_large(count, max_rows, side_hint=f"iceberg table {spec.table!r}")
    df = read_iceberg_dataframe(cfg, secret, spec.table, limit=max_rows + 1, table=table)
    if len(df) > max_rows:
        raise _too_large(len(df), max_rows, side_hint=f"iceberg table {spec.table!r}")
    return df

content.text: That's the kind of bug that would silently break Flow D in prod. The fix is to make it **impossible to forget** — one helper that resolves *both* credentials, used by every call site.

input.command:

import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py"); s = p.read_text()

helper = '''

def iceberg_credentials(
    config: IcebergConfig, secret_ref: str | None, secret_store: SecretStore
) -> tuple[str | None, str | None]:
    """``(storage_secret, catalog_secret)`` for an Iceberg connection.

    **The one place both credentials are resolved.** An Iceberg SQL catalog needs two
    (the storage key AND the catalog DB password, #754/#826), and `catalog_uri` no
    longer carries the second one — so a caller that resolves only the storage secret
    would connect to the catalog with *no password* and fail obscurely, or worse,
    succeed against an unauthenticated catalog. Every read path (runner, profiler,
    comparison reader) goes through here so none of them can forget.

    Both are optional: a local warehouse / vended-credentials REST catalog has neither.
    """
    secret = secret_store.get(secret_ref) if secret_ref else None
    catalog_secret = (
        secret_store.get(config.catalog_secret_name) if config.catalog_secret_name else None
    )
    return secret, catalog_secret

'''
# insert before build_iceberg_runner
i = s.index("def build_iceberg_runner(")
s = s[:i] + helper.lstrip("\n") + "\n" + s[i:]

# build_iceberg_runner uses it
s = s.replace('''    iceberg_config = IcebergConfig.model_validate(config)
    secret = secret_store.get(secret_ref) if secret_ref else None
    # The SQL-catalog DB password lives in the SecretStore under a NAME held in
    # config — the URI itself stays credential-less at rest (#754/#826).
    catalog_secret = (
        secret_store.get(iceberg_config.catalog_secret_name)
        if iceberg_config.catalog_secret_name
        else None
    )
    return IcebergCheckRunner(config=iceberg_config, secret=secret, catalog_secret=catalog_secret)''',
'''    iceberg_config = IcebergConfig.model_validate(config)
    secret, catalog_secret = iceberg_credentials(iceberg_config, secret_ref, secret_store)
    return IcebergCheckRunner(config=iceberg_config, secret=secret, catalog_secret=catalog_secret)''', 1)
p.write_text(s)
print("helper added")
PY

class TestEveryReadPathGetsTheCatalogCredential:
    """Regression: `catalog_uri` is credential-less now, so ANY read path that resolves
    only the storage secret would connect to the catalog with no password (#754/#826).
    The runner path was wired first and the profiler/comparison paths were NOT — this
    pins that every one of them goes through `iceberg_credentials`."""

    class _Store:
        def get(self, ref: str) -> str:
            return {"kv-storage": "STORAGE_KEY", "kv-catalog": "CATALOG_PW"}[ref]

    def _cfg(self) -> IcebergConfig:
        return IcebergConfig(
            catalog_type="sql",
            catalog_uri="postgresql+psycopg2://u@h:5432/cat",
            catalog_secret_name="kv-catalog",
            secret_property="adls.account-key",
        )

    def test_resolves_both_credentials(self) -> None:
        secret, catalog_secret = iceberg_credentials(self._cfg(), "kv-storage", self._Store())
        assert secret == "STORAGE_KEY"
        assert catalog_secret == "CATALOG_PW"

    def test_both_are_optional(self) -> None:
        cfg = IcebergConfig(catalog_type="rest", catalog_uri="https://cat.example")
        assert iceberg_credentials(cfg, None, self._Store()) == (None, None)

    def test_the_runner_actually_reaches_the_catalog_with_the_password(
        self, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
    ) -> None:
        seen: dict[str, Any] = {}

        def _fake_load_catalog(name: str, **props: Any) -> Any:
            seen.update(props)
            raise RuntimeError("stop here — we only care about the props")

        monkeypatch.setattr("pyiceberg.catalog.load_catalog", _fake_load_catalog)
        runner = build_iceberg_runner(
            config=self._cfg().model_dump(),
            secret_ref="kv-storage",
            secret_store=self._Store(),
        )
        with pytest.raises(RuntimeError):
            runner._load_table("retail.orders")

        # The DSN handed to pyiceberg carries the catalog password…
        assert seen["uri"] == "REDACTED"
        # …and the storage key still lands on its own property.
        assert seen["adls.account-key"] == "STORAGE_KEY"
EOF
