=== dbt profiles.yml (how OL derives the dataset namespace) ===
# dbt profile — fully env-var driven so the same file works for local `dbt build`
# (source ../secrets.sh) and the container host (env injected by the ACA job / compose).
# No secrets in this file. The vars are the same ones the Airflow loader uses
# (_harness.py load_to_snowflake) — SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT/USER/PASSWORD/WAREHOUSE/DATABASE/ROLE.
#
# ROLE is the least-privilege DATAQ_LOADER (snowflake.tf) — NOT ACCOUNTADMIN. It
# owns the two dbt-created schemas (needs CREATE SCHEMA on the database), so it can
# create the views + dynamic tables and grant SELECT/USAGE to DATAQ_READER
# (on-run-end hooks). Dynamic tables also require change tracking on the RETAIL base
# tables — pre-enabled in snowflake.tf + bootstrap_change_tracking.sql because the
# loader doesn't own them. We override SNOWFLAKE_ROLE here (it's ACCOUNTADMIN in
# secrets.sh for the Airflow/ADF loads) so a stray env doesn't silently re-escalate.
# target.schema is the marts default; staging overrides to ANALYTICS_STG in dbt_project.yml.
dataq_lineage:
  target: dev
  outputs:
    dev:
      type: snowflake
      account: "{{ env_var('SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT') }}"
      user: "{{ env_var('SNOWFLAKE_USER') }}"
      password: <redacted>
      role: "{{ env_var('DBT_SNOWFLAKE_ROLE', 'DATAQ_LOADER') }}"
      # `or` fallback (not just env_var's default) because secrets.sh exports these
      # SET-BUT-EMPTY, and env_var only substitutes the default when a var is UNSET.
      warehouse: "{{ env_var('SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE', '') or 'DATAQ_WH' }}"
      database: "{{ env_var('SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE', '') or 'DATAQ_DB' }}"
      schema: "ANALYTICS"
      threads: 4
      client_session_keep_alive: false
=== dbt_project.yml name ===
name: "dataq_lineage"
profile: "dataq_lineage"

content.text: While CI finishes, let me file the silent-lineage-outage finding — that's the systemic bug behind #823.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
gh issue create --title "Lineage went silently dark for 6 days on an expired credential — a failing orchestration poll has no user-visible signal" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --label bug --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What happened

Prod has had **no lineage at all** for six days, and nothing told anyone.

Root cause, from App Insights: the **dbt** orchestration poll has been failing every 10 minutes with an expired ADLS SAS:

```
orchestration_poll_failed  provider=dbt
ClientAuthenticationError: AuthenticationFailed
  Signed expiry time [Mon, 06 Jul 2026 14:03:00 GMT] must be after signed start time [...]
```

The dbt provider polls `run_results.json` / `manifest.json` from the artifacts store; on a successful build it refreshes `lineage_edges` (#759). With the credential expired, that read throws, the poll aborts, and the **whole dbt-manifest lineage path is dark** — which is the *shipped* lineage path, independent of any catalog.

This is what actually made the asset lineage graph empty in prod. (The Marquez/`LineageProvider` investigation in #823 was a red herring: even a perfect catalog pull wouldn't have helped, because the manifest path — the one that works — was broken underneath it.)

## The real defect: it is invisible

The poll logs `orchestration_poll_failed` at ERROR and moves on. There is **no user-visible signal anywhere**:

- The connection still shows as configured; nothing marks it unhealthy.
- The Assets/lineage UI renders the *normal empty state* ("No lineage recorded for this asset") — **identical to "this asset genuinely has no lineage"**. A broken integration and a legitimately-isolated asset are indistinguishable.
- No alert fires. `poll_orchestration_runs` reports `errors: 3` in its own summary and still returns success.

So a feature can rot for a week and the product cheerfully reports "nothing to see here". That's the bug worth fixing — the expired SAS is just the trigger.

This is the same failure shape as the one flagged in #823 ("make 'every seed unavailable' distinguishable from 'the catalog genuinely has no lineage'"), which suggests it's a *class* of problem in the lineage/orchestration surfaces, not a one-off.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A repeatedly-failing orchestration poll surfaces **in the product**, not only in logs — e.g. connection health goes degraded (we now have a connection-health axis, #803), and/or an alert fires after N consecutive failures
- [ ] "Lineage unavailable because the source is failing" is **visually distinct** from "this asset has no lineage" in the UI — never show a clean empty state over a broken integration
- [ ] An expiring credential is surfaced *before* it expires (the SAS expiry is known at mint time)
- [ ] Regression test: a poll whose artifact read raises marks the connection unhealthy rather than silently succeeding

## Immediate ops action (separate from the fix)
Re-mint the ADLS SAS for the dbt artifacts connection and re-point the connection secret.

Found while investigating why prod showed no lineage after the #801–#805 deploy.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

content.text:
Let me summarize where we are, since this turn has uncovered a lot.

## You were right — the lineage is ours, and I found the actual cause

It was **not** the Marquez/dbt-ol producer gap I blamed. That was a red herring. The real cause, from App Insights:

```
orchestration_poll_failed  provider=dbt
ClientAuthenticationError: Signed expiry time [Mon, 06 Jul 2026 14:03:00 GMT] ...
```

**The ADLS SAS expired on 6 July.** DataQ therefore couldn't read `manifest.json`/`run_results.json`, the dbt provider poll has been dying every 10 minutes for **six days**, and the *shipped* dbt-manifest lineage path (#759) — the one that actually works, independent of any catalog — has been dark the whole time. Even a perfect Marquez pull wouldn't have helped, because the path underneath it was broken.

**The deeper defect (filed as #828):** it was completely invisible. The UI showed the *normal* empty state — "No lineage recorded for this asset" — which is **indistinguishable from an asset that genuinely has no lineage**. No alert, no unhealthy connection, and `poll_orchestration_runs` still reported success while logging `errors: 3`. A feature rotted for a week and the product said "nothing to see here". That's the same failure shape I flagged in #823, so it's a class of problem, not a one-off.

## Security work (PR #827, CI running)

I fixed the credential leak **at the root**, not with redaction:

- **Iceberg gets a second credential slot.** ADR 0030 assumed one secret per connection; a SQL catalog needs two (storage key + catalog DB password), which is *why* the password got smuggled into `catalog_uri`. Now `catalog_secret_name` holds a SecretStore *key name* (an identifier, safe in config), the URI ships credential-less, and the password is injected at catalog-load only.
- **A validator rejects a password in `catalog_uri` outright** — refuse it at the door rather than redact forever.
- **`asset_identity` strips credentials**, so the OL namespace can never carry one. Bonus: the identity is now **stable across a password rotation** (previously, rotating it silently *forked the asset* and orphaned its lineage/incidents).
- **Read API scrubs** any URI password from any config value, any type (defence in depth for legacy rows).
- **Migration `c9d0e1f2a3b4`** repairs poisoned rows **in place** so `assets.id` and every FK survive; tested up→down→up against real Postgres.

**My own review caught a real bug in my change:** I'd wired the catalog secret into the check-runner but *not* the profiler or comparison reader — they'd have connected with **no password**. Fixed at the right altitude: one `iceberg_credentials()` resolver every read path goes through, so none can forget. Gate: 2,122 tests, 98.22%, bandit clean.

## Still to do (in order)

1. Merge + deploy #827.
2. **Rotate the Postgres password**, store it in Key Vault, re-point the iceberg connection.
3. **Re-mint the expired ADLS SAS** → this is what actually restores lineage in prod.
4. Verify: no credential in the UI, iceberg profiling works, lineage graph populated.

Note that step 2 has a sharp edge I'm sequencing around: the migration strips the password from the connection config, so Iceberg reads will fail until I re-point it. The harness is down, so nothing is running against it in the meantime.

**`{"ok": true}`** — the connection test passed against the **rotated** password. That proves the entire new mechanism end-to-end in production: DataQ resolved both credentials (storage key + the new catalog password from Key Vault), injected the password into the credential-less URI at load time, and reached the SQL catalog. The old password is dead and nothing is broken.

## Your question: why the full connection string?

You've spotted a real design smell, and the honest answer is **two different things got conflated**.

**The identity has to be the physical location.** That namespace is the ADR 0034 OpenLineage identity, and its whole purpose is to be *globally* stable and to match byte-for-byte what other OL producers (dbt, Spark, Airflow) emit for the same table. A friendly name can't do that job:
- it isn't global — two connections can point at the same physical catalog, and they must resolve to **one** asset, not two;
- it isn't stable — renaming a connection would silently fork every asset under it (exactly the bug I just fixed for password rotation);
- and no external OL producer knows your connection names, so lineage would never join.

**But you're right that it shouldn't be what you *read*.** Displaying the raw DSN as a tree-root label is just bad UI — it's long, it leaks host/username detail into the page, and it tells a human nothing useful. `snowflake://PVQSOEQ-ZGB34383` and `unitycatalog://dbc-4492…` are barely better.

So the fix is a **display label**, not a different identity: keep the OL namespace as the key, and render the *connection name* (e.g. `harness-iceberg`, `snowflake-dev`) with the namespace demoted to a tooltip/subtitle. That's purely presentational and also shrinks the incidental info exposure.

That needs a small backend addition (the asset doesn't currently carry a connection reference — assets are connection-agnostic by ADR 0034, so it'd be a "one or more connections resolve to this namespace" lookup).

Want me to do that now, or file it and finish the lineage restoration first? My instinct is **lineage first** (prod has had none for six days), then the label — but say the word and I'll flip the order.

input.new_string:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AssetSummary:
    """List-row aggregation for one visible asset.

    **Two orthogonal health axes (#803).** The old single "health" conflated them:

    - *Suite health* (data quality, ADR 0005) — ``worst_severity`` over the
      **evaluated** checks, plus ``checks_total``/``checks_passed``. Operational
      results never rank here, so a datasource DataQ couldn't reach reads as "no
      data", never as a green "passing" nor as a red data failure.
    - *Connection health* (reachability) — ``has_operational_error`` /
      ``has_skip``: could DataQ execute against the datasource at all? Derived
      **purely from the runs already recorded** (a latest run that `failed`, or
      any ``error``/``skip`` result on one) — there is no connection-probe polling
      loop behind this, by design.
    """

    id: uuid.UUID
    namespace: str
    name: str
    env: str | None
    description: str | None
    owner_user_id: uuid.UUID | None
    last_seen: datetime
    suite_count: int
    # ── suite health (data quality) ──
    worst_severity: str | None
    checks_total: int
    checks_passed: int
    last_run_at: datetime | None
    # ── connection health (reachability / execution) ──
    # `has_failed_run`: any latest run whose *execution* `failed` (wrote no results).
    # `has_active_run`: any latest run still `queued`/`running` (hasn't concluded).
    # `has_operational_error`: a failed run OR any `error` result — DataQ could not
    #   evaluate against the datasource. `has_skip`: any `skip` result (a
    #   precondition, e.g. the batch hasn't landed, wasn't met) — degraded, not down.
    has_failed_run: bool = False
    has_active_run: bool = False
    has_operational_error: bool = False
    has_skip: bool = False

) -> list[ComposingSuite]:
    """Build the per-suite breakdown for one asset's suites (sorted by name)."""
    composing: list[ComposingSuite] = []

def _run_outcome(
    run: Run | None,
    outcome: tuple[int, int, str | None] | None,
    op_flags: tuple[bool, bool] | None = None,
) -> RunOutcome:
    """Assemble a `RunOutcome` from a suite's latest run + its check-outcome tuple
    + its operational (`error`/`skip`) flags."""
    if run is None:
        return RunOutcome()
    total, passed, worst = outcome or (0, 0, None)
    has_error, has_skip = op_flags or (False, False)
    return RunOutcome(
        run_id=run.id,
        status=run.status,
        worst_severity=worst,
        checks_total=total,
        checks_passed=passed,
        finished_at=run.finished_at,
        created_at=run.created_at,
        has_error=has_error,
        has_skip=has_skip,
    )


def _composing_suites(
    suites: list[Suite],
    levels: dict[uuid.UUID, str | None],
    latest_runs: dict[uuid.UUID, Run],
    outcomes: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[int, int, str | None]],
    op_flags: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[bool, bool]] | None = None,
) -> list[ComposingSuite]:
    """Build the per-suite breakdown for one asset's suites (sorted by name)."""
    op_flags = op_flags or {}
    composing: list[ComposingSuite] = []
    for suite in suites:
        level = levels.get(suite.id)
        if level is None:  # defensive: only reachable suites are passed in
            continue
        run = latest_runs.get(suite.id)
        outcome = outcomes.get(run.id) if run is not None else None
        flags = op_flags.get(run.id) if run is not None else None
        composing.append(
            ComposingSuite(
                suite_id=suite.id,
                name=suite.name,
                my_permission=level,
                latest_run=_run_outcome(run, outcome, flags),
            )
        )
    return composing

input.new_string:
class AssetSummaryRead(ApiModel):
    """List-row aggregation for one visible asset, carrying **two orthogonal health
    axes** (#803) the UI renders separately:

    - *Suite health* (data quality) — `worst_severity` / `checks_*` over the
      **evaluated** checks of the caller-visible composing suites' latest runs;
      `worst_severity` is null when all passed or nothing has run. Operational
      results never rank here.
    - *Connection health* (reachability) — `has_operational_error` / `has_skip`
      (plus the execution states below): could DataQ execute against the
      datasource at all? Derived from the recorded runs only — no connection-probe
      polling loop.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)

    id: uuid.UUID
    namespace: str
    name: str
    env: str | None
    description: str | None
    owner_user_id: uuid.UUID | None
    last_seen: datetime
    suite_count: int
    worst_severity: str | None
    checks_total: int
    checks_passed: int
    last_run_at: datetime | None
    # Latest-run execution states (distinct from check severity): any composing
    # suite's latest run `failed` / still `queued`/`running`.
    has_failed_run: bool
    has_active_run: bool
    # Connection health (#803): a failed run OR any `error` result → DataQ could not
    # evaluate against the datasource; `skip` → a precondition wasn't met (degraded).
    has_operational_error: bool
    has_skip: bool

# ── connection health vs suite health (#803) ─────────────────────────────────
#
# The two axes must not bleed into each other: operational `error`/`skip` results
# (#122) feed *connection* health (could DataQ reach the datasource?) and are
# invisible to *suite* health (is the data good?), which is severity-only.


def _suite_with_run(db: Any, owner: User, *, run_status: str, result_statuses: list[str]) -> Asset:
    """A suite on a fresh asset with one run carrying `result_statuses` results."""
    conn = _conn(db, owner)
    suite = suite_service.create_suite(
        db,
        name=f"S-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
        description=None,
        connection_id=conn.id,
        created_by=owner.id,
        target={"table": f"T{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}"},
    )
    run = Run(suite_id=suite.id, status=run_status, triggered_by="manual")
    db.add(run)
    db.flush()
    for status in result_statuses:
        check = Check(
            suite_id=suite.id,
            name=f"c-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
            expectation_type="expect_column_to_exist",
            kwargs={"column": "X"},
        )
        db.add(check)
        db.flush()
        db.add(Result(run_id=run.id, check_id=check.id, status=status))
    db.commit()
    assert suite.asset_id is not None
    return db.get(Asset, suite.asset_id)


def test_error_result_feeds_connection_health_not_suite_health(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A run that SUCCEEDED but whose check threw: connection health is degraded
    (operational error) while suite health stays severity-free — the exact case
    `has_failed_run` alone misses, since the run itself never failed."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["error"])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_operational_error is True  # connection axis: could not evaluate
    assert s.has_failed_run is False  # the run itself succeeded
    assert s.worst_severity is None  # suite axis: no DQ verdict at all
    assert s.checks_total == 0  # `error` is not an evaluated check


def test_skip_result_is_degraded_not_an_error(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """`skip` = a precondition wasn't met (the batch hasn't landed). The run
    executed, so it is NOT an operational error — only a degraded connection."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["skip"])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_skip is True
    assert s.has_operational_error is False
    assert s.worst_severity is None
    assert s.checks_total == 0


def test_failed_run_is_an_operational_error(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A run whose execution failed wrote no results at all — connection axis."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="failed", result_statuses=[])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_failed_run is True
    assert s.has_operational_error is True
    assert s.worst_severity is None


def test_failing_data_does_not_touch_connection_health(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """The mirror case: the datasource was perfectly reachable, the DATA is bad.
    Suite health goes red; connection health stays clean."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(
        db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["pass", "critical"]
    )
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

            label="Connection"
            health={connectionHealth(summary)}
            hint="Whether DataQ could reach and execute against the datasource behind this asset."
          />
          <HealthAxis
            label="Data quality"
            health={suiteHealth(summary)}
            hint="The severity-weighted verdict of the suites on this asset. Operational errors are excluded."
          />
        </Flex>

input.old_string:
- **Asset browse** — two lenses over the same assets: a **By source** drill-down
  (datasource → database/catalog → schema → table, with env + health on each leaf) and a
  flat, searchable **All assets** table. Both open the asset detail (health across suites,

input.new_string:
- **Asset browse** — two lenses over the same assets: a **By source** drill-down
  (datasource → database/catalog → schema → table, with env + health on each leaf) and a
  flat, searchable **All assets** table. Both open the asset detail (health across suites,
  lineage, incidents).
- **Two health axes on an asset** — *Connection health* ("could DataQ reach and execute
  against the datasource?" — a failed run, or a check the datasource threw on) and
  *Data-quality health* (the severity-weighted verdict of its suites). They are derived
  and shown separately, so a datasource DataQ cannot connect to never masquerades as a
  data failure — and a run that evaluated nothing reads **No data**, never a green
  "passing". Operational `error`/`skip` results feed only the connection axis. Both are
  read off the runs already recorded — there is no connection-probe polling loop.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(assets): split asset health into connection vs data-quality axes (#803)

The single asset "Health" conflated two different questions: could DataQ reach
the datasource at all, and is the data any good. A datasource DataQ could not
connect to therefore rendered as a data failure.

Split them into two derived, separately-labelled axes on AssetDetail:

- Connection health — could DataQ execute against the datasource? A latest run
  whose execution `failed`, or any check the datasource threw on (`error`), reads
  Errors; a `skip` (precondition unmet, e.g. the batch hasn't landed) reads
  Degraded; a clean concluded run reads Reachable.
- Data-quality health — the ADR 0005 severity-weighted verdict of the suites.
  Operational `error`/`skip` results never rank here (#122 semantics).

Backend: `operational_result_flags` (the exact complement of
`check_outcome_counts`, which drops skip/error) rolls up to two new
AssetSummary fields, `has_operational_error` + `has_skip`. Derived entirely from
runs already recorded — no connection-probe polling loop, by design.

Suite health now keys "Passing" off `checks_total > 0` (evaluated checks) rather
than "a run happened", so a run that evaluated nothing reports "No data" instead
of a green Passing it hasn't earned. The dashboard needs-attention rule moves to
`has_operational_error`, which additionally catches a run that *succeeded* while
its checks errored — a case the old run-status-only rule read as healthy.

Fixes #803

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
git push -u origin feature/issue-803-asset-health-split -q 2>&1 | tail -1
gh pr create --title "feat(assets): split asset health into connection vs data-quality axes (#803)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What & why

Fixes #803. The asset **Health** signal conflated two different questions — *could DataQ reach the datasource?* and *is the data good?* — so a datasource DataQ couldn't connect to rendered as a data failure.

Live proof of the split (the seeded ORDERS asset is **both** unreachable *and* has bad data — previously you'd only ever have seen "Critical"):

> **Connection** `Errors`  ·  **Data quality** `Critical`

## The two axes

- **Connection health** — could DataQ *execute* against the datasource? A latest run whose execution `failed`, or any check the datasource threw on (`error`) → **Errors**; a `skip` (a precondition wasn't met — the batch hasn't landed) → **Degraded**; a clean concluded run → **Reachable**; nothing run → **No runs**.
- **Data-quality health** — the ADR 0005 severity-weighted verdict of the suites. Operational `error`/`skip` results never rank here (#122 semantics).

**Derivation (documented, no new polling loop):** both read straight off the runs already recorded. New `run_service.operational_result_flags` is the exact complement of `check_outcome_counts` (which deliberately drops `skip`/`error`); those dropped rows *are* the signal that DataQ couldn't evaluate. They roll up into two new `AssetSummary` fields, `has_operational_error` + `has_skip`.

## Two bugs this surfaced and fixes
- Suite health now keys **"Passing" off `checks_total > 0`** (evaluated checks), not "a run happened" — so a run that evaluated nothing reports **No data**, not a green Passing it hasn't earned.
- The dashboard needs-attention rule moves to `has_operational_error`, which also catches a run that **succeeded while its checks errored** — the old run-status-only rule read that as healthy.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Two clearly-labelled health indicators on AssetDetail (+ tooltips); lead-strip attention rule updated
- [x] Connection-health derivation documented (service/schema/health.ts docstrings + docs/features.md); **no new polling loop**
- [x] Operational `error`/`skip` results feed connection health, not suite health (#122)

## Testing
- Backend: 5 new service tests pinning the separation — a *succeeded* run whose check `error`ed (connection Errors, suite untouched, the case `has_failed_run` alone misses), `skip` = degraded-not-error, failed run = operational error, and the mirror case (bad data must **not** dirty connection health). Full suite 2092 passed, 98.28%.
- Frontend: 11 new `connectionHealth`/`suiteHealth` unit tests (incl. the "No data, never green" invariant) + 2 AssetDetail tests. Full suite 514 passed.
- Full local gate green: black, ruff, mypy, eslint, prettier, tsc. Verified live against the local stack (screenshot above).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

result.output: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`high effort → 8 inline angles → dedup (no verify) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** in sequence yourself, in THIS context — do NOT spawn subagents for them. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates are the dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Dedup only (no verify)

Pool all candidates. Dedup near-duplicates only (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). Do NOT run verifiers; do NOT re-judge. Sort by severity.

## Output

Target **at least 5 findings**. If fewer genuine findings exist, emit what you have — do not invent to hit the floor.

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.

+def operational_result_flags(
+    session: Session, run_ids: Sequence[uuid.UUID]
+) -> dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[bool, bool]]:
+    """Per-run ``(has_error, has_skip)`` over its **operational** results (#122).
+
+    The exact complement of :func:`check_outcome_counts`, which counts only the
+    *evaluated* severity tiers and deliberately drops ``skip``/``error``. Those
+    dropped rows are the signal that DataQ could not evaluate a check — the
+    datasource threw (``error``) or a precondition wasn't met (``skip``) — so the
+    asset view reads them to derive **connection** health (can we reach the thing?)
+    separately from **suite** health (is the data good?), per #803.
+
+    Presence, not counts: one grouped query, a row exists iff that status occurs.
+    """
+    if not run_ids:
+        return {}
+    rows = session.execute(
+        select(Result.run_id, Result.status)
+        .where(Result.run_id.in_(run_ids), Result.status.in_(RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES))
+        .group_by(Result.run_id, Result.status)
+    ).all()
+    flags: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[bool, bool]] = {}
+    for run_id, status in rows:
+        has_error, has_skip = flags.get(run_id, (False, False))
+        flags[run_id] = (has_error or status == "error", has_skip or status == "skip")
+    return flags
+
+

+# ── connection health vs suite health (#803) ─────────────────────────────────
+#
+# The two axes must not bleed into each other: operational `error`/`skip` results
+# (#122) feed *connection* health (could DataQ reach the datasource?) and are
+# invisible to *suite* health (is the data good?), which is severity-only.
+
+
+def _suite_with_run(db: Any, owner: User, *, run_status: str, result_statuses: list[str]) -> Asset:
+    """A suite on a fresh asset with one run carrying `result_statuses` results."""
+    conn = _conn(db, owner)
+    suite = suite_service.create_suite(
+        db,
+        name=f"S-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
+        description=None,
+        connection_id=conn.id,
+        created_by=owner.id,
+        target={"table": f"T{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}"},
+    )
+    run = Run(suite_id=suite.id, status=run_status, triggered_by="manual")
+    db.add(run)
+    db.flush()
+    for status in result_statuses:
+        check = Check(
+            suite_id=suite.id,
+            name=f"c-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
+            expectation_type="expect_column_to_exist",
+            config={"column": "X"},
+        )
+        db.add(check)
+        db.flush()
+        db.add(Result(run_id=run.id, check_id=check.id, status=status))
+    db.commit()
+    assert suite.asset_id is not None
+    return db.get(Asset, suite.asset_id)

+def test_error_result_feeds_connection_health_not_suite_health(db_session: Any) -> None:
+    """A run that SUCCEEDED but whose check threw: connection health is degraded
+    (operational error) while suite health stays severity-free — the exact case
+    `has_failed_run` alone misses, since the run itself never failed."""
+    owner = _user(db_session)
+    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["error"])
+    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)
+
+    assert s.has_operational_error is True  # connection axis: could not evaluate
+    assert s.has_failed_run is False  # the run itself succeeded
+    assert s.worst_severity is None  # suite axis: no DQ verdict at all
+    assert s.checks_total == 0  # `error` is not an evaluated check
+
+
+def test_skip_result_is_degraded_not_an_error(db_session: Any) -> None:
+    """`skip` = a precondition wasn't met (the batch hasn't landed). The run
+    executed, so it is NOT an operational error — only a degraded connection."""
+    owner = _user(db_session)
+    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["skip"])
+    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)
+
+    assert s.has_skip is True
+    assert s.has_operational_error is False
+    assert s.worst_severity is None
+    assert s.checks_total == 0
+
+
+def test_failed_run_is_an_operational_error(db_session: Any) -> None:
+    """A run whose execution failed wrote no results at all — connection axis."""
+    owner = _user(db_session)
+    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="failed", result_statuses=[])
+    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)
+
+    assert s.has_failed_run is True
+    assert s.has_operational_error is True
+    assert s.worst_severity is None
+
+
+def test_failing_data_does_not_touch_connection_health(db_session: Any) -> None:
+    """The mirror case: the datasource was perfectly reachable, the DATA is bad.
+    Suite health goes red; connection health stays clean."""
+    owner = _user(db_session)
+    asset = _suite_with_run(
+        db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["pass", "critical"]
+    )
+    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)
+
+    assert s.worst_severity == "critical"  # suite axis: data is bad
+    assert s.checks_total == 2 and s.checks_passed == 1
+    assert s.has_operational_error is False  # connection axis: nothing wrong here
+    assert s.has_skip is False

+/**
+ * **Connection health (#803)** — *could DataQ reach and execute against the
+ * datasource behind this asset?* Says nothing about whether the data is good.
+ *
+ * Derivation (no polling loop — read straight off the runs already recorded):
+ * - a latest run whose execution `failed`, or any check that `error`ed (the
+ *   datasource threw) → **Errors**;
+ * - any `skip` (a precondition wasn't met — e.g. the batch hasn't landed) →
+ *   **Degraded**: it executed, it just found nothing to check;
+ * - a run in flight → **Running**; a clean concluded run → **Reachable**;
+ * - nothing has ever run → **No runs** (unknown, not healthy).
+ */
+export function connectionHealth(
+  summary: Pick<
+    AssetSummary,
+    'has_operational_error' | 'has_skip' | 'has_active_run' | 'last_run_at'
+  >,
+): Health {
+  if (summary.has_operational_error) return { label: 'Errors', color: 'error' };
+  if (summary.has_skip) return { label: 'Degraded', color: 'warning' };
+  if (summary.has_active_run) return STATUS_HEALTH.running;
+  if (summary.last_run_at !== null) return { label: 'Reachable', color: 'success' };
+  return { label: 'No runs', color: 'default' };
+}
+
+/**
+ * **Suite health (#803)** — the ADR 0005 severity-weighted verdict of the suites
+ * on this asset. Purely about the *data*; operational `error`/`skip` results are
+ * excluded (they're `connectionHealth`'s business, per #122).
+ *
+ * Crucially this keys "Passing" off `checks_total > 0` — the count of *evaluated*
+ * checks — not off "a run happened". A run that failed operationally, or one whose
+ * checks all skipped, evaluated nothing, so it reports **No data** rather than a
+ * green "Passing" it hasn't earned (the bug the old single signal had once the
+ * operational flags were pulled out of it).
+ */
+export function suiteHealth(
+  summary: Pick<AssetSummary, 'worst_severity' | 'checks_total' | 'has_active_run' | 'last_run_at'>,
+): Health {
+  if (summary.worst_severity) return SEVERITY_HEALTH[summary.worst_severity];
+  if (summary.has_active_run) return STATUS_HEALTH.running;
+  if (summary.checks_total > 0) return { label: 'Passing', color: 'success' };
+  if (summary.last_run_at !== null) return { label: 'No data', color: 'default' };
+  return { label: 'No runs', color: 'default' };
+}
+
 /** Per-suite health from its latest run (execution status + severity). */
 export function runHealth(run: RunOutcome): Health {
   if (run.run_id === null) return { label: 'No runs', color: 'default' };

post "frontend/src/components/assets/health.ts" 75 '**[medium · correctness]** A **`cancelled`** latest run falls through to a green **Reachable** here, even though nothing proved the datasource was reachable.

The asset rollup (`_roll_up`) only flags `failed` and `queued`/`running` — a `cancelled` run sets neither `has_failed_run` nor `has_active_run`, and (if the user cancelled before any check ran) writes no `error`/`skip` results either. So the summary looks identical to a clean success: `has_operational_error=false`, `has_skip=false`, `has_active_run=false`, `last_run_at` set → **Reachable**.

Failure scenario: user cancels a queued run against a datasource that is in fact down. Connection health renders green "Reachable".

Note `runHealth` (per-suite, which sees `run.status`) *does* handle `cancelled` — only the asset-level rollup has no cancelled signal. Fix would be a `has_cancelled_run` on `AssetSummary` (mirroring the other two execution flags), mapped to a neutral "Cancelled"/"Unknown" here rather than green. Pre-existing at the rollup level (the old `assetHealth` had the same hole), so not a regression — but the split is exactly the moment to close it.'

post "frontend/src/components/assets/health.ts" 95 '**[low · correctness]** Same root cause as the `cancelled` note above, on the data-quality axis: a run **cancelled part-way** can still report a green **Passing**.

Failure scenario: a suite with 5 checks is cancelled after 2 have passed. The rollup gives `checks_total=2, checks_passed=2, worst_severity=null`, no active/failed flag → `checks_total > 0` → **Passing**. The asset reads green on a run that never finished evaluating the other 3 checks.

`checks_total > 0` is a strictly better gate than the old "a run happened" (it correctly catches the evaluated-nothing case), so this is an improvement, not a regression — but a `has_cancelled_run` flag would let it say "Partial"/"Cancelled" instead of green.'

post "backend/tests/services/test_asset_view_service.py" 192 '**[low · test-quality]** Passing `None` as the `Session` with a `# type: ignore` only works because of the `if not run_ids` early return — the test is coupled to that guard rather than to the behaviour.

If someone reorders the guard below the query, this test crashes with an `AttributeError` on `None` instead of failing with a clear message. Passing the real `db_session` fixture (and asserting `== {}`) tests the same empty-input contract without the coupling.'

    has_failed_run: bool = False

input.new_string:
def test_cancelled_run_is_flagged_so_it_never_rolls_up_green(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A cancelled run proves nothing: killed before a check ran, we may never have
    reached the datasource. It is neither a failure nor an active run, so without
    its own flag it would look identical to a clean success and read green on both
    axes. (The UI keys `connectionHealth`/`suiteHealth` off this.)"""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="cancelled", result_statuses=[])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_cancelled_run is True
    # Explicitly NOT any of the other execution states — this is the whole point:
    # nothing else in the summary distinguishes it from a healthy run.
    assert s.has_failed_run is False
    assert s.has_active_run is False
    assert s.has_operational_error is False
    assert s.checks_total == 0

input.new_string:
def downstream_assets(session: Session, asset_id: uuid.UUID, *, max_depth: int = 10) -> list[Asset]:
    """Distinct downstream assets of ``asset_id`` in BFS order (blast radius)."""
    return [asset for asset, _depth in _walk(session, asset_id, "down", max_depth)]


def upstream_assets(session: Session, asset_id: uuid.UUID, *, max_depth: int = 10) -> list[Asset]:
    """Distinct upstream assets of ``asset_id`` in BFS order (provenance)."""
    return [asset for asset, _depth in _walk(session, asset_id, "up", max_depth)]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class LineageNeighbourhood:
    """The lineage subgraph around one asset — enough to *draw* it (#805).

    The flat `upstream_assets` / `downstream_assets` lists answer "what is reachable",
    which is all the blast-radius consumers need. A graph view additionally needs to
    know **how far** each node sits (to lay it out in hop columns) and **which node
    connects to which** (to draw a truthful edge rather than a guessed one) — so this
    carries the hop distance per node and the edges actually traversed.

    ``edges`` are normalized ``(upstream_id, downstream_id)`` pairs regardless of the
    direction they were discovered in, so the two walks compose into one DAG.
    """

    upstream: list[tuple[Asset, int]]
    downstream: list[tuple[Asset, int]]
    edges: list[tuple[uuid.UUID, uuid.UUID]]


def lineage_neighbourhood(
    session: Session, asset_id: uuid.UUID, *, max_depth: int = 10
) -> LineageNeighbourhood:
    """Both walks from ``asset_id``, with hop depth per node + the traversed edges."""
    up, up_edges = _walk_graph(session, asset_id, "up", max_depth)
    down, down_edges = _walk_graph(session, asset_id, "down", max_depth)

    ids = [aid for aid, _ in up] + [aid for aid, _ in down]
    by_id = (
        {a.id: a for a in session.scalars(select(Asset).where(Asset.id.in_(ids)))} if ids else {}
    )
    return LineageNeighbourhood(
        upstream=[(by_id[aid], d) for aid, d in up if aid in by_id],
        downstream=[(by_id[aid], d) for aid, d in down if aid in by_id],
        edges=sorted(set(up_edges) | set(down_edges)),
    )


def _walk(
    session: Session, start: uuid.UUID, direction: str, max_depth: int
) -> list[tuple[Asset, int]]:
    """`_walk_graph`, resolved to `Asset` rows (dropping the edges)."""
    order, _edges = _walk_graph(session, start, direction, max_depth)
    if not order:
        return []
    ids = [aid for aid, _ in order]
    by_id = {a.id: a for a in session.scalars(select(Asset).where(Asset.id.in_(ids)))}
    return [(by_id[aid], depth) for aid, depth in order if aid in by_id]


def _walk_graph(
    session: Session, start: uuid.UUID, direction: str, max_depth: int
) -> tuple[list[tuple[uuid.UUID, int]], set[tuple[uuid.UUID, uuid.UUID]]]:
    """Depth-capped BFS over `lineage_edges` in ``direction`` from ``start``.

    Source-agnostic (blast radius spans every lineage source, not just dbt).
    De-duplicates and caps at ``max_depth`` hops, returning the distinct reachable
    asset ids **with their hop distance** in discovery (BFS) order, plus every edge
    traversed — including cross-edges back into already-visited nodes, so the
    subgraph is faithful and not just a spanning tree.

    Edges come back normalized as ``(upstream_id, downstream_id)`` whichever way we
    walked, so an up-walk and a down-walk can be unioned into one DAG.
    """
    if direction == "down":
        from_col, to_col = LineageEdge.upstream_asset_id, LineageEdge.downstream_asset_id
    else:
        from_col, to_col = LineageEdge.downstream_asset_id, LineageEdge.upstream_asset_id

    visited = {start}
    frontier = [start]
    order: list[tuple[uuid.UUID, int]] = []
    edges: set[tuple[uuid.UUID, uuid.UUID]] = set()
    depth = 0
    while frontier and depth < max_depth:
        rows = session.execute(select(from_col, to_col).where(from_col.in_(frontier))).all()
        next_frontier: list[uuid.UUID] = []
        for src, dst in rows:
            # `src`/`dst` are in walk order; store the edge in its true direction.
            edges.add((src, dst) if direction == "down" else (dst, src))
            if dst not in visited:
                visited.add(dst)
                order.append((dst, depth + 1))
                next_frontier.append(dst)
        frontier = next_frontier
        depth += 1
    return order, edges

content.text: The dbt fixture gives a 2-hop chain — ideal. Let me add the neighbourhood test.

input.new_string:
# ── neighbourhood graph: hop depth + real edges (#805) ───────────────────────


def test_neighbourhood_carries_hop_depth_and_the_real_edges(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """The graph view needs more than "what is reachable": it needs how far each
    node sits (to lay it out in hop columns) and which node connects to which (to
    draw a truthful edge rather than a guessed one)."""
    conn = _connection(db_session, env="dev")
    _anchor(db_session, name=_ORDERS_HEADER, env="dev")
    refresh_dbt_edges(db_session, connection=conn, graph=_graph("v1"))

    orders_header = _asset_id(db_session, _ORDERS_HEADER)
    stg_orders = _asset_id(db_session, _STG_ORDERS)
    graph = lineage_neighbourhood(db_session, orders_header)

    # Depth: the staging view is one hop out, the marts it feeds are two.
    depth_by_name = {a.name: d for a, d in graph.downstream}
    assert depth_by_name[_STG_ORDERS] == 1
    assert depth_by_name[_MART_REVENUE] == 2
    assert depth_by_name[_MART_CUSTOMER] == 2
    assert graph.upstream == []  # orders_header is a source

    # Edges are real (upstream → downstream), so the UI can draw mart_revenue as
    # hanging off stg_orders — not off orders_header, which it never touches.
    mart_revenue = _asset_id(db_session, _MART_REVENUE)
    assert (orders_header, stg_orders) in graph.edges
    assert (stg_orders, mart_revenue) in graph.edges
    assert (orders_header, mart_revenue) not in graph.edges


def test_neighbourhood_walks_both_directions_from_the_middle(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """From a node in the middle of the chain, both walks run and their edges union
    into one DAG (the up-walk's edges are normalized to upstream→downstream too)."""
    conn = _connection(db_session, env="dev")
    _anchor(db_session, name=_ORDERS_HEADER, env="dev")
    refresh_dbt_edges(db_session, connection=conn, graph=_graph("v1"))

    stg_orders = _asset_id(db_session, _STG_ORDERS)
    orders_header = _asset_id(db_session, _ORDERS_HEADER)
    graph = lineage_neighbourhood(db_session, stg_orders)

    assert {a.name for a, _ in graph.upstream} == {_ORDERS_HEADER}
    assert {a.name for a, _ in graph.downstream} == {_MART_REVENUE, _MART_CUSTOMER}
    # Discovered by walking UP, but still stored upstream→downstream.
    assert (orders_header, stg_orders) in graph.edges


def test_neighbourhood_of_an_isolated_asset_is_empty(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A 0-edge asset yields an empty graph — the UI's empty-state case."""
    _anchor(db_session, name=_ORDERS_HEADER, env="dev")
    graph = lineage_neighbourhood(db_session, _asset_id(db_session, _ORDERS_HEADER))
    assert graph.upstream == [] and graph.downstream == [] and graph.edges == []


# ── fail-soft: no namespace anchor ────────────────────────────────────────────

git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(assets): left-to-right lineage graph replacing the two list boxes (#805)

AssetDetail rendered upstream and downstream as two separate list boxes. Replace
them with one left-to-right DAG: provenance on the left, the asset under view
centred, blast radius on the right, one column per hop. Nodes are clickable
(navigate to that asset) and keyboard-operable; the centre is labelled but not
actionable.

The flat BFS lists only answered "what is reachable". A graph also needs to know
how far each node sits (to lay it out in hop columns) and which node connects to
which (to draw a truthful edge rather than a guessed one), so `lineage.edges`
gains `lineage_neighbourhood`: the same depth-capped BFS, now returning each
node's hop depth plus the edges it actually traversed (normalized
upstream→downstream, cross-edges included, so the subgraph is faithful and not
just a spanning tree). Surfaced as `depth` on LineageNodeRead and a new
`lineage_edges` on AssetDetailRead. A depth-2 node is therefore drawn hanging off
the depth-1 node it truly descends from, never off the centre it has no edge to.

No new dependency: the layout is a layered DAG we place ourselves (pure,
unit-tested `lineageLayout.ts`) rendered as inline SVG. That keeps the ADR 0031
licence surface at zero and makes the mobile requirement simple — the graph
scrolls inside its own card and the page never gains a horizontal scrollbar
(pinned by a Playwright assertion at 390px), which a pan/zoom canvas would make
harder rather than easier.

Fixes #805

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
gh pr create --title "feat(assets): left-to-right lineage graph replacing the two list boxes (#805)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --base main --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What & why

Fixes #805. AssetDetail rendered lineage as two separate list boxes. It's now **one left-to-right graph** — provenance left, the asset under view centred, blast radius right, **one column per hop** — with clickable, keyboard-operable nodes.

> Live against the local dbt chain, centred on `MART_ORDER_REVENUE`: two depth-1 staging views, two depth-2 raw tables, and **crossing edges** (`ORDERS_HEADER` feeds *both* staging views) — which is the proof the edges are real rather than guessed.

## The backend bit that makes it honest

The flat BFS lists only answered *"what is reachable"*. A graph also needs **how far** each node sits (to lay it out in hop columns) and **what connects to what** (to draw a truthful edge). So `lineage.edges` gains `lineage_neighbourhood`: the same depth-capped BFS, now returning each node's **hop depth** plus the **edges it actually traversed** — normalized `upstream → downstream` whichever way we walked, cross-edges included, so the subgraph is faithful and not just a spanning tree.

Surfaced as `depth` on `LineageNodeRead` and a new `lineage_edges` on `AssetDetailRead`. A depth-2 node is therefore drawn hanging off the depth-1 node it truly descends from — **never off the centre it has no edge to**, which a naive 3-column render would have implied.

## On the dependency

The issue suggested reactflow. I went with **no new dependency**: the layout is a layered DAG we can place ourselves (pure, unit-tested `lineageLayout.ts`) rendered as inline SVG. Reasons:
- reactflow wouldn't auto-layout anyway (that needs dagre/elk on top) — we'd compute the same x/y ourselves.
- It keeps the **ADR 0031 / rule-40 licence surface at zero** — nothing new to audit.
- It makes the mobile AC *simpler*: an SVG in an `overflow-x` container scrolls inside its card natively, where a pan/zoom canvas fights the page.

Easy to swap later if you want pan/zoom/minimap — the layout module is already the seam. Happy to reverse if you'd rather have the library.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Graph view with directional layout + clickable nodes (upstream → asset → downstream, arrows, hop columns, depth ≥2)
- [x] Handles 0-edge assets gracefully (empty state preserved — no empty canvas)
- [x] Mobile: horizontal scroll inside the card, **no page overflow** (asserted at 390px)
- [x] Dependency licence checked — **none added**

## Testing
- Backend: 3 new `lineage_neighbourhood` tests (hop depth per node; edges are real — `(orders_header, mart_revenue)` explicitly asserted **absent**; both-direction walk from mid-chain; isolated asset). Full suite **2096 passed, 98.29%**.
- Frontend: 7 pure layout tests (columns, sibling stacking, bezier endpoints, dangling-edge drop, canvas sizing) + 7 component tests (edge count, click→open, depth-2 reachable, keyboard Enter, centre not actionable, scroll container, empty state). Full suite **533 passed**; new files 100% lines.
- E2E: `lineage-graph.spec.ts` — directional graph + node navigation, and the 390px mobile scroll/no-overflow assertion. Green against the live local stack.
- Full local gate: black, ruff, mypy, eslint, prettier, tsc.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
echo "--- lineageLayout.ts ---" && grep -n "for (const n of downstream)\|const byId = new Map" frontend/src/components/assets/lineageLayout.ts
echo "--- LineageGraph.tsx ---" && grep -n "stroke={node.isCenter\|function leafName" frontend/src/components/assets/LineageGraph.tsx
echo "--- assetTree nameSegments exists? ---" && grep -n "export function nameSegments" frontend/src/components/assets/assetTree.ts

cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)

post "frontend/src/components/assets/lineageLayout.ts" 96 '**[medium · correctness]** A **cycle** in the lineage graph puts the same asset in *both* `upstream` and `downstream`, and this lays it out twice — two `LaidOutNode`s sharing one `id`.

Consequences: `byId` (line 136) silently keeps only the last one, so edges may anchor to the wrong copy; and `LineageGraph` renders `key={n.id}` per node, so React gets **duplicate keys** and draws the asset in two columns at once.

Failure scenario: `lineage_edges` contains A→B and B→A (nothing enforces acyclicity — the per-direction BFS is cycle-safe via its own `visited` set, but the up-walk and the down-walk are independent, and a catalog like Marquez can absolutely emit a cycle). Open A: B is returned as upstream *and* downstream → duplicate node.

Fix: dedupe by id when building `placed` — first occurrence wins (or prefer the smaller |depth|), e.g. skip any node whose id is already placed (the centre included, so a self-edge can'"'"'t duplicate the centre either).'

post "frontend/src/components/assets/LineageGraph.tsx" 171 '**[medium · accessibility]** Monitored vs unmonitored is now conveyed by **border colour alone** (`#91caff` vs `BRAND.border`). The list boxes this replaces carried an explicit `Monitored`/`Unmonitored` **tag per node**.

WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Colour): colour must not be the only visual means of conveying information — this is invisible to a colour-blind user and to anyone on a low-contrast screen, and the legend at the bottom only explains what the colour *means*, it doesn'"'"'t make any individual node readable.

The node also no longer states it in its accessible name, so a screen-reader user gets `Open asset X` with no monitored signal at all. Cheap fixes: append it to the `aria-label` (`Open asset X (monitored)`), and add a small dot/badge glyph or a differing stroke-dash to the node so it is distinguishable without colour.'

post "frontend/src/components/assets/LineageGraph.tsx" 193 '**[low · reuse]** `leafName` re-implements the slash-vs-dot segment split that `assetTree.ts` already exports as `nameSegments` (added in #802, same directory):

```ts
import { nameSegments } from '"'"'./assetTree'"'"';
const leafName = (name: string) => nameSegments(name).at(-1) ?? name;
```

Two copies of the "how is an OL asset name segmented" rule will drift — that rule is exactly the kind of thing that changes once (e.g. when an Iceberg namespace form shows up) and then only gets fixed in one place.'

    low-urgency daily tick as the sample-failures and orphan-asset sweeps. Callable
    on-demand too (`refresh_lineage_pull.delay()`). Returns the live pulled-edge count
    (0 when unconfigured or nothing pulled); `refresh_pulled_edges` fails open per step.
    """
    provider = lineage_pull.get_lineage_provider()
    if provider is None:
        return 0
    session = get_session()
    try:
        return lineage_pull.refresh_pulled_edges(session, provider=provider) or 0
    finally:
        session.close()

content.text: There's already a `LineageProvider` section in `docs/orchestration.md` with the compose steps. Let me read it and add the verified end-to-end runbook.

result.output:
84	### Lineage from a catalog — the `LineageProvider` seam (ADR 0034, #762)
85	
86	The dbt slice above sees only what the dbt manifest models. A **governance catalog** sees
87	more — including consumers that emit no OpenLineage themselves (a Power BI report now sitting
88	downstream of a monitored mart). DataQ pulls that graph through a provider-agnostic
89	**`LineageProvider`** seam (mirroring the `OrchestrationProvider` discipline — no
90	provider-specific branching in service code), caching the pulled edges into the same
91	`lineage_edges` table with `source='marquez'`. The seam's graph carries a **node kind** per node
92	(`dataset` today; `job` collapsed through; `bi_report`/`dashboard` reserved) — so **downstream
93	nodes are not assumed to be tables**, and a BI/dashboard node round-trips the moment a
94	capable catalog (Purview/DataHub) lands behind the seam, with no schema or query change.
95	
96	- **Reference implementation: Marquez** (Apache-2.0). Pull = `GET {MARQUEZ_URL}/api/v1/lineage?
97	  nodeId=dataset:{namespace}:{name}&depth=N`; identity matches DataQ assets byte-for-byte because
98	  both use the OpenLineage naming spec. Fail-soft (5 s timeout, node cap, depth clamp) — and a
99	  dead catalog is treated as **unavailable**, not as empty lineage: the refresh skips pruning for
100	  that pass (an outage can never wipe the cached graph), never surfaces an error, and stale edges
101	  wait for the next clean pull.
102	- **Deferred behind the same seam** (do not build): **DataHub** (8 GB-RAM footprint — bring your
103	  own; our emitter already feeds its OL receiver), **OpenMetadata** (SDK is source-available,
104	  blocked by ADR 0031; REST-only would need its own ADR), **Purview** (Azure-only, parked).
105	- **Config (dark by default).** Set `LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez` + `MARQUEZ_URL=http://marquez:5000`
106	  in `.env.app`. The daily **`refresh_lineage_pull`** beat task then seeds a pull from each known
107	  asset, collapses jobs to dataset→dataset edges, and upserts them. Pulled edges carry a **NULL
108	  `connection_id`** (a catalog pull has no orchestration connection) and dedupe on the
109	  `(upstream, downstream, source) WHERE connection_id IS NULL` partial unique index; their prune is
110	  scoped to `(source='marquez', connection_id IS NULL)`, so **dbt-sourced edges are never touched**
111	  — the two sources coexist as distinct rows for the same table pair, and the blast-radius walk
112	  spans both.
113	
114	**Local round-trip (emitter → Marquez → pull).** The reference compose stack ships a Marquez
115	(+ its own Postgres) behind an opt-in profile so a plain `docker compose up` is unaffected:
116	
117	```bash
118	# 1. start Marquez (2 extra containers) alongside the app
119	docker compose --profile lineage up
120	# 2. in .env.app, point the emitter and the pull at Marquez, then restart api+worker:

122	#      LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez
123	#      MARQUEZ_URL=http://marquez:5000
124	# 3. run a suite (emits START/COMPLETE RunEvents to Marquez), then trigger the pull:
125	docker compose exec worker python -c \
126	  "from backend.app.worker.tasks import refresh_lineage_pull; print(refresh_lineage_pull())"
127	# 4. the pulled edges appear in lineage_edges with source='marquez'.
128	```

content.text: The existing runbook has a real gap I just discovered: **DataQ's emitter only emits the validated dataset as a job *input*** — so "run a suite" alone yields *no* dataset→dataset edges. Let me correct the runbook with what I actually verified.

input.old_string:
**Local round-trip (emitter → Marquez → pull).** The reference compose stack ships a Marquez
(+ its own Postgres) behind an opt-in profile so a plain `docker compose up` is unaffected:

```bash
# 1. start Marquez (2 extra containers) alongside the app
docker compose --profile lineage up
# 2. in .env.app, point the emitter and the pull at Marquez, then restart api+worker:
#      OPENLINEAGE_URL=http://marquez:5000/api/v1/lineage
#      LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez
#      MARQUEZ_URL=http://marquez:5000
# 3. run a suite (emits START/COMPLETE RunEvents to Marquez), then trigger the pull:
docker compose exec worker python -c \
  "from backend.app.worker.tasks import refresh_lineage_pull; print(refresh_lineage_pull())"
# 4. the pulled edges appear in lineage_edges with source='marquez'.
```
input.new_string:
**Local round-trip (producer → Marquez → pull → graph).** The reference compose stack ships a
Marquez (+ its own Postgres) behind an opt-in profile so a plain `docker compose up` is unaffected.

> **DataQ's own emission is not enough to produce lineage — and that is by design.** A DataQ run
> *reads* the asset it validates, so the emitter (#758) sends it as a job **input** and emits no
> outputs. That makes the DQ job a consumer in the catalog; it creates **no dataset→dataset edge**.
> Edges come from whatever actually *transforms* data — dbt's OpenLineage integration, a Spark or
> Airflow OL emitter, or any producer posting a RunEvent with both `inputs` and `outputs`. Point
> Marquez at one of those (or post the events yourself, as below) and the pull has a graph to find.

```bash
# 1. start Marquez (2 extra containers) alongside the app; it serves on host :5002
docker compose --profile lineage up -d marquez-db marquez
curl -sf http://localhost:5002/api/v1/namespaces   # ready check

# 2. get a producer chain INTO Marquez. Any OL producer will do; to verify the seam
#    without one, post RunEvents whose inputs/outputs use the SAME OpenLineage
#    namespace + name as your DataQ assets (that identity match is the whole point —
#    see ADR 0034). One event per transformation, e.g.
#       {namespace}: snowflake://<account>
#       ORDERS_HEADER -> STG_ORDERS -> MART_ORDER_REVENUE -> BI dashboard
curl -X POST http://localhost:5002/api/v1/lineage -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{
  "eventType": "COMPLETE", "eventTime": "2026-07-12T19:05:00.000Z",
  "run": {"runId": "<uuid>"}, "job": {"namespace": "dbt-local", "name": "build_stg_orders"},
  "inputs":  [{"namespace": "snowflake://ACCT", "name": "DB.RETAIL.ORDERS_HEADER"}],
  "outputs": [{"namespace": "snowflake://ACCT", "name": "DB.ANALYTICS_STG.STG_ORDERS"}],
  "producer": "https://example/local-verify"}'

# 3. point the pull at Marquez (dark until you do) and refresh. In .env.app:
#      LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez
#      MARQUEZ_URL=http://marquez:5000        # in-network; :5002 is host-side only
#    (add OPENLINEAGE_URL=http://marquez:5000/api/v1/lineage too if you also want
#     DataQ's own DQ-job events in the catalog — they add no edges, see the note above)
docker compose exec worker python -c \
  "from backend.app.worker.tasks import refresh_lineage_pull; print(refresh_lineage_pull())"

# 4. the pulled edges land in lineage_edges with source='marquez' (dbt edges untouched),
#    and render in the asset's lineage graph (#805).
docker compose exec postgres psql -U dataq -d dataq \
  -c "select source, count(*) from lineage_edges group by source;"
```

**Verified locally 2026-07-12 (#804).** Off-by-default confirmed (`get_lineage_provider()` → `None`
with no env); env-configured, the pull walked each known asset as a seed, **cached 3
`source='marquez'` edges** alongside the existing 8 `source='dbt'` ones (neither pruned the other),
and the asset's lineage graph rendered the full chain — including a **BI dashboard node that only
Marquez knew about**, which dbt's manifest never produced. Seeds that Marquez has never heard of
log a fail-soft `marquez_lineage_pull_failed` warning per node and are skipped, exactly as intended.

**Not yet verified against a deployed app.** The Azure subscription lapsed (2026-07-12), so there is
no running prod to attach a catalog to, and the harness Flow-A chain needs live Snowflake. The
prod-side stand-up — a Marquez the deployed app can reach, plus enabling OL emission on the harness
flows — is deferred until infra returns; the app side needs no further change (it is one env var).

producer → Marquez → LineageProvider pull → lineage_edges → the asset graph —
then corrected the runbook where it was misleading.

The runbook implied that running a DataQ suite would give you lineage. It won't,
and that is by design: a DataQ run *reads* the asset it validates, so the #758
emitter sends it as a job INPUT and emits no outputs. That makes the DQ job a
consumer in the catalog and creates no dataset→dataset edge. Edges come from
whatever actually transforms data (dbt's OL integration, Spark/Airflow emitters,
or any producer posting inputs+outputs). The runbook now says so and shows how to
post a producer chain to verify the seam without one.

Verified locally: off-by-default confirmed (no env → provider is None);
env-configured, the pull seeded from each known asset and cached 3
`source='marquez'` edges alongside the existing 8 `source='dbt'` ones (neither
source pruned the other), and the lineage graph rendered the full chain including
a BI dashboard node only Marquez knew about. Unknown seeds fail soft per node.

Prod stand-up stays deferred: the Azure subscription lapsed 2026-07-12, so there
is no deployed app to attach a catalog to and Flow-A needs live Snowflake. The
app side needs no further change — it is one env var.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
git push -u origin docs/issue-804-marquez-local-verify -q 2>&1 | tail -1
gh pr create --title "docs(lineage): correct + verify the local Marquez round-trip runbook (#804)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What & why

Fixes #804 (the local half; prod deferred — see below).

Brought up the compose `lineage` profile and drove the **whole loop end to end**: producer → Marquez → `LineageProvider` pull → `lineage_edges` → the asset lineage graph. Then fixed the runbook where it was actively misleading.

## The runbook bug this found

The old runbook said *"run a suite (emits START/COMPLETE RunEvents to Marquez), then trigger the pull → the pulled edges appear"*. **They don't** — and that's by design:

> A DataQ run *reads* the asset it validates, so the #758 emitter sends it as a job **input** and emits no outputs. That makes the DQ job a *consumer* in the catalog; it creates **no dataset→dataset edge**.

Edges come from whatever actually **transforms** data — dbt's OL integration, a Spark/Airflow emitter, or any producer posting a RunEvent with both `inputs` and `outputs`. Anyone following the old steps would have concluded the seam was broken. The runbook now states this and shows how to post a producer chain to verify the seam without standing a producer up.

## Verified locally (2026-07-12)
- **Off by default** — `get_lineage_provider()` → `None` with no env set.
- **Env-configured** — the pull seeded from each known asset and cached **3 `source='marquez'` edges** alongside the existing **8 `source='dbt'`** ones; **neither source pruned the other** (the partial-index + source-scoped prune from #762 holds).
- **Rendered in the UI** — the asset's lineage graph showed the full chain, including a **BI dashboard node only Marquez knew about**, which dbt's manifest never produced. That node is the proof the pull (not dbt) fed the graph.
- **Fail-soft** — seeds Marquez has never heard of log one `marquez_lineage_pull_failed` warning per node and are skipped, leaving the cache intact.

Local stack restored afterwards (synthetic edges + the BI asset removed, Marquez containers stopped).

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Marquez reachable from the app (env-configured, **off by default**) — both states verified
- [x] ≥2 lineage edges in the UI — **3** pulled edges, chain rendered in the graph
- [x] Runbook section — corrected + verified (`docs/orchestration.md`)
- [ ] ~Prod/harness stand-up~ — **deferred**, see below

## What is deferred (and why)

The Azure subscription lapsed **2026-07-12**, so there is no deployed app to attach a catalog to, and the harness Flow-A chain needs live Snowflake. The prod-side stand-up (a Marquez the deployed app can reach + OL emission on the harness flows) waits for infra to return. **The app side needs no further change — it is one env var.** Happy to keep #804 open for that half if you'd rather; say the word and I'll reopen/split it.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

content.text: Everything green on the clean replay. Let me repoint the PR branch and force-push.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(assets): left-to-right lineage graph replacing the two list boxes (#805)

AssetDetail rendered upstream and downstream as two separate list boxes. Replace
them with one left-to-right DAG: provenance on the left, the asset under view
centred, blast radius on the right, one column per hop. Nodes are clickable
(navigate to that asset) and keyboard-operable; the centre is labelled but not
actionable.

The flat BFS lists only answered "what is reachable". A graph also needs to know
how far each node sits (to lay it out in hop columns) and which node connects to
which (to draw a truthful edge rather than a guessed one), so `lineage.edges`
gains `lineage_neighbourhood`: the same depth-capped BFS, now returning each
node's hop depth plus the edges it actually traversed (normalized
upstream→downstream, cross-edges included, so the subgraph is faithful and not
just a spanning tree). Surfaced as `depth` on LineageNodeRead and a new
`lineage_edges` on AssetDetailRead. A depth-2 node is therefore drawn hanging off
the depth-1 node it truly descends from, never off the centre it has no edge to.

Cycle-safe: nothing enforces an acyclic graph and the two walks are independent,
so an asset can come back as BOTH upstream and downstream — each is placed once
(duplicate ids would mean duplicate React keys and mis-anchored edges).

No new dependency: the layout is a layered DAG we place ourselves (pure,
unit-tested `lineageLayout.ts`) rendered as inline SVG. That keeps the ADR 0031
licence surface at zero and makes the mobile requirement simple — the graph
scrolls inside its own card and the page never gains a horizontal scrollbar
(pinned by a Playwright assertion at 390px). "Monitored" is not colour-only
(WCAG 1.4.1): it is in the node's accessible name and marked with a dot glyph.

Fixes #805

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.new_string:
pipeline_run_count = _seed_pipeline_runs(session, connections=connections)
    incident_count = _seed_incidents(session, suite=first_suite) if first_suite else 0
    edge_count = _seed_lineage(session, suite=first_suite) if first_suite else 0

    session.commit()
    return {
        "connections": len(connections),
        "suites": suite_count,
        "checks": check_count,
        "shares": 1,
        "runs": run_count,
        "pipeline_runs": pipeline_run_count,
        "incidents": incident_count,
        "lineage_edges": edge_count,
    }


# The demo lineage chain around the shared ORDERS asset. Two hops each way, so the
# asset's graph (#805) has real depth-≥2 provenance AND blast radius to lay out —
# without it the lineage view has nothing to show on a fresh install, and its e2e
# would have nothing to assert.
#
#   RAW.ORDERS_RAW ─┐
#                   ├─► ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.STG_ORDERS ─► ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.ORDERS ─► …MART.REVENUE ─► …BI.REVENUE_DAILY
#   RAW.CUSTOMERS ──┘
_DEMO_LINEAGE: tuple[tuple[str, str], ...] = (
    ("ANALYTICS.RAW.ORDERS_RAW", "ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.STG_ORDERS"),
    ("ANALYTICS.RAW.CUSTOMERS", "ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.STG_ORDERS"),
    ("ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.STG_ORDERS", "ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.ORDERS"),
    ("ANALYTICS.PUBLIC.ORDERS", "ANALYTICS.MART.REVENUE"),
    ("ANALYTICS.MART.REVENUE", "ANALYTICS.BI.REVENUE_DAILY"),
)


def _seed_lineage(session: Session, *, suite: Suite) -> int:
    """Seed a lineage neighbourhood around the suite's asset (idempotent).

    Anchored on the *resolved* asset's namespace so the seeded neighbours share its
    OpenLineage identity space (ADR 0034) — the same rule the dbt/Marquez importers
    follow, so a real import later dedupes against these rather than forking them.
    Edges are tagged ``source='dbt'`` (the manifest importer's provenance) and carry
    the suite's connection, matching what `refresh_dbt_edges` would have written.
    """
    if suite.asset_id is None:
        return 0
    anchor = session.get(Asset, suite.asset_id)
    if anchor is None:
        return 0

    names = {n for edge in _DEMO_LINEAGE for n in edge}
    asset_ids = upsert_assets(
        session,
        [
            {
                "namespace": anchor.namespace,
                "name": name,
                "env": anchor.env,
                "connection_id": suite.connection_id,
            }
            for name in sorted(names)
        ],
        preserve_provenance=True,
    )

    seeded = 0
    for upstream, downstream in _DEMO_LINEAGE:
        up_id = asset_ids[(anchor.namespace, upstream)]
        down_id = asset_ids[(anchor.namespace, downstream)]
        exists = session.scalar(
            select(LineageEdge.id).where(
                LineageEdge.upstream_asset_id == up_id,
                LineageEdge.downstream_asset_id == down_id,
                LineageEdge.source == "dbt",
            )
        )
        if exists is not None:
            continue
        session.add(
            LineageEdge(
                upstream_asset_id=up_id,
                downstream_asset_id=down_id,
                source="dbt",
                connection_id=suite.connection_id,
            )
        )
        seeded += 1
    session.flush()
    return seeded

git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(assets): refetch on asset→asset navigation + seed a demo lineage chain (#805)

Two things the CI E2E caught that local runs didn't.

1. Stale detail on asset→asset navigation. `useAsyncData` fetches on mount (and
   on reload) only, so clicking a lineage node changed the URL but re-rendered the
   SAME AssetDetail instance — leaving the previous asset's data on screen under
   the new id. Nothing could hit this before: the old lineage list boxes were not
   clickable, so #805's graph is the first asset→asset navigation in the app. Key
   the page on the route param so the id is the identity of the mount. Regression
   test pins it.

2. The E2E depended on a lineage chain that only existed in my local DB (loaded
   from a dbt manifest), so it passed locally and failed on CI's fresh seed. Fix
   the real gap rather than the test: the demo seed now lands a lineage
   neighbourhood around the shared ORDERS asset (RAW ×2 → STG → ORDERS → MART →
   BI), giving depth-≥2 provenance AND blast radius. Without it a fresh install
   has no lineage to show at all. The spec now reaches the asset the way a user
   does (via the tree), never by a hardcoded id.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

10	Before committing and pushing changes, run the relevant local checks first. Do not rely on CI to discover format/lint/type/test failures.

12	For frontend changes: `pnpm format:check && pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck` (and `pnpm test` once tests exist).
13	For backend changes: `black --check backend/ && ruff check backend/ && mypy backend/app/ && bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app/` (and `pytest` once tests exist). Run inside the conda env (`conda run -n dataq ...`); the base interpreter lacks the deps.
14	
15	**Bandit is a separate CI gate (`Backend — SAST`) that Ruff does NOT cover.** Ruff `# noqa: S105` does not silence Bandit's `B105` — they are independent. PR #74 (4a) passed Ruff locally but failed CI Bandit on `B105 hardcoded_password_string` for a constant whose *name* contained "token". Prefer renaming to avoid the password wordlist (token/secret/pass/pwd/key) over dual `# noqa: S105` + `# nosec B105` suppression.
16	
17	**Local bandit clean ≠ CI bandit clean (version divergence, PR #807 2026-07-12):** local bandit didn't flag f-string SQL inside `sa.text(...)` (B608) and even warned my `# nosec B608` was unused — the CI-pinned bandit DID flag it and failed the gate. When intentionally writing something bandit-adjacent (interpolated SQL, secret-wordlist names), keep BOTH `# noqa: S6xx` and `# nosec B6xx` with a justification comment even if local bandit calls the nosec unused; the "unused nosec" is a warning, the missing one is a CI failure.
18

User said: "failed again, please check locally if possible before commit (we are spamming it now)." Re-confirmed after PR1 close-out: "going forward lets check in local when possible then."
